rows.english: Again, the indeterminateness of root-causeless consciousness is established without it. And the profitableness, etc., of those with root-cause is bound up with wise attention, etc., not with the associated root-causes.

rows.pali: Yadi ca sampayuttahetūsu sabhāvatova kusalādibhāvo siyā, sampayuttesu hetupaṭibaddho alobho kusalo vā siyā abyākato vā.
rows.english: And if the profitableness, etc., resided in the associated root-causes as an individual essence, then either the non-greed bound up with the root-cause in the associated states would be only profitable or it would be only indeterminate;

rows.pali: Yasmā pana ubhayathāpi hoti, tasmā yathā sampayuttesu, evaṃ hetūsupi kusalāditā pariyesitabbā.
rows.english: but since it can be both, profitableness, etc., in the root-causes must still be sought for, just as in the associated states [such as wise attention, and so on].

rows.pali: Kusalādibhāvasādhanavasena pana hetūnaṃ mūlaṭṭhaṃ agahetvā suppatiṭṭhitabhāvasādhanavasena gayhamāne na kiñci virujjhati.
rows.english: 70.But when the root-causes’ sense of root is taken as establishing stableness, rather than as establishing profitableness, etc., there is no contradiction.

rows.pali: Laddhahetupaccayā hi dhammā virūḷhamūlā viya pādapā thirā honti suppatiṭṭhitā, ahetukā tilabījakādisevālā viya na suppatiṭṭhitā.
rows.english: For states that have obtained a root-cause condition are firm, like trees, and stable; but those without root-cause are, like moss [with roots no bigger than] sesame seeds, etc., unstable.

rows.pali: Iti mūlaṭṭhena upakārakoti suppatiṭṭhitabhāvasādhanena upakārako dhammo hetupaccayoti veditabbo.
rows.english: So an assistantial state may be understood as a root-cause condition, since it establishes stableness through being of assistance in the sense of a root.

rows.pali: 596.Tato paresu ārammaṇabhāvena upakārako dhammo ārammaṇapaccayo.
rows.english: 71.(2) As to the others that follow, a state that assists by being an object is an object condition.

rows.pali: So "rūpāyatanaṃ cakkhuviññāṇadhātuyā"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.2) ārabhitvāpi "yaṃ yaṃ dhammaṃ ārabbha ye ye dhammā uppajjanti cittacetasikā dhammā, te te dhammā tesaṃ tesaṃ dhammānaṃ ārammaṇapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.2) osāpitattā na koci dhammo na hoti.
rows.english: Now, there are no states that are not object conditions; for the passage beginning “The visible-data base [is a condition, as object condition,] for the eye-consciousness element” concludes thus: “When any states, as states of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants, arise contingent upon any states, these [latter] states are conditions, as object condition, for those [former] states” (Paṭṭh I 1).11

rows.pali: Yathā hi dubbalo puriso daṇḍaṃ vā rajjuṃ vā ālambitvāva uṭṭhahati ceva tiṭṭhati ca, evaṃ cittacetasikā dhammā rūpādiārammaṇaṃ ārabbheva uppajjanti ceva tiṭṭhanti ca.
rows.english: For just as a weak man both gets up and stands by hanging on to (ālambitvā) a stick or rope, so states of consciousness and consciousness- concomitants always arise and are present contingent upon visible data, etc., as their object (ārammaṇa = ālambana).

rows.pali: Tasmā sabbepi cittacetasikānaṃ ārammaṇabhūtā dhammā ārammaṇapaccayoti veditabbā.
rows.english: Therefore all states that are objects of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants should be understood as object condition. [534]

rows.pali: 597.Jeṭṭhakaṭṭhena upakārako dhammo adhipatipaccayo, so sahajātārammaṇavasena duvidho.
rows.english: 72. (3) A state that assists in the sense of being foremost is a predominance condition. It is of two kinds as conascent and as object.

rows.pali: Tattha "chandādhipati chandasampayuttakānaṃ dhammānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ adhipatipaccayena paccayo"tiādivacanato (paṭṭhā. 1.3.3) chandavīriyacittavīmaṃsāsaṅkhātā cattāro dhammā adhipatipaccayoti veditabbā, no ca kho ekato.
rows.english: Herein, because of the passage beginning “Predominance of zeal is a condition, as predominance condition, for states associated with zeal and for the kinds of materiality originated thereby” (Paṭṭh I 2), it is the four states called zeal, [purity of] consciousness, energy, and inquiry, that should be understood as predominance condition; but not simultaneously,

rows.pali: Yadā hi chandaṃ dhuraṃ chandaṃ jeṭṭhakaṃ katvā cittaṃ pavattati, tadā chandova adhipati, na itare.
rows.english: for when consciousness occurs with emphasis on zeal and putting zeal foremost, then it is zeal and not the others that is predominant.

rows.pali: Esa nayo sesesupi.
rows.english: So with the rest.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana dhammaṃ garuṃ katvā arūpadhammā pavattanti, so nesaṃ ārammaṇādhipati.
rows.english: But the state, by giving importance to which, immaterial states occur, is their object-predominance.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "yaṃ yaṃ dhammaṃ garuṃ katvā ye ye dhammā uppajjanti cittacetasikā dhammā, te te dhammā tesaṃ tesaṃ dhammānaṃ adhipatipaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.3).
rows.english: Hence it is said: “When any states, as states of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants, arise by giving importance to any states, these [latter] states are a condition, as predominance condition, for those [former] states” (Paṭṭh I 2).

rows.pali: 598.Anantarabhāvena upakārako dhammo anantarapaccayo.
rows.english: 73. (4), (5) A state that assists by being proximate is a proximity condition.

rows.pali: Samanantarabhāvena upakārako dhammo samanantarapaccayo.
rows.english: A state that assists by being contiguous is a contiguity condition.

rows.pali: Idañca paccayadvayaṃ bahudhā papañcayanti.
rows.english: The explanation of this pair of conditions is very diffuse,

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panettha sāro, yo hi esa cakkhuviññāṇānantarā manodhātu, manodhātuanantarā manoviññāṇadhātūtiādi cittaniyamo, so yasmā purimapurimacittavaseneva ijjhati, na aññathā, tasmā attano attano anantaraṃ anurūpassa cittuppādassa uppādanasamattho dhammo anantarapaccayo.
rows.english: but substantially it is this:12 the regular order of consciousness begins thus, mind element is proximate (next) after eye- consciousness, mind-consciousness element is proximate (next) after mind element, and this is established only by each preceding consciousness, not otherwise; consequently, a state that is capable of arousing an appropriate kind of consciousness proximate (next) to itself is a proximity condition.

rows.pali: Tenevāha – "anantarapaccayoti cakkhuviññāṇadhātu taṃsampayuttakā ca dhammā manodhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ anantarapaccayena paccayo"tiādi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.4).
rows.english: Hence it is said: “Proximity condition: eye-consciousness and the states associated therewith are a condition, as proximity condition, for mind element and for the states associated therewith” (Paṭṭh I 2).

rows.pali: Yo anantarapaccayo, sveva samanantarapaccayo.
rows.english: 74.(5) Proximity condition is the same as contiguity condition.

rows.pali: Byañjanamattameva hettha nānaṃ, upacayasantatīsu viya adhivacananiruttidukādīsu viya ca.
rows.english: The difference here is only in the letter, just as in the case of the words “growth” and “continuity” (XIV.66), etc., and as in the case of the “terminology dyad,” “language dyad,” (Dhs §1306) and so on;

rows.pali: Atthato pana nānaṃ natthi.
rows.english: there is none in the meaning.

rows.pali: Yampi "atthānantaratāya anantarapaccayo, kālānantaratāya samanantarapaccayo"ti ācariyānaṃ mataṃ, taṃ "nirodhā vuṭṭhahantassa nevasaññānāsaññāyatanakusalaṃ phalasamāpattiyā samanantarapaccayena paccayo"tiādīhi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.418) virujjhati.
rows.english: 75. The opinion of [certain] teachers13 is that proximity condition refers to proximity of aim (fruit) and contiguity condition refers to proximity of time. But that is contradicted by such statements as “The profitable [consciousness] belonging to the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception in one who emerges from cessation is a condition, as contiguity condition, for fruition attainment [consciousness]” (Paṭṭh I 160).

rows.pali: Yampi tattha vadanti "dhammānaṃ samuṭṭhāpanasamatthatā na parihāyati, bhāvanābalena pana vāritattā dhammā samanantarā nuppajjantī"ti, tampi kālānantaratāya abhāvameva sādheti.
rows.english: 76. Now, they say in this context that “the ability of states to produce [their fruit] is not diminished, but the influence of meditative development prevents states from arising in proximity.” But that only establishes that there is no proximity of time;

rows.pali: Bhāvanābalena hi tattha kālānantaratā natthīti, mayampi etadeva vadāma.
rows.english: and we also say the same, namely, that there is no proximity of time there owing to the influence of development.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca kālānantaratā natthi, tasmā samanantarapaccayatā na yujjati.
rows.english: But since there is no proximity of time, the state of contiguity condition is therefore impossible [according to them]

rows.pali: Kālānantaratāya hi tesaṃ samanantarapaccayo hotīti laddhi.
rows.english: since their belief is that the contiguity condition depends on proximity of time (cf. M-a II 363).

rows.pali: Tasmā abhinivesaṃ akatvā byañjanamattatovettha nānākaraṇaṃ paccetabbaṃ, na atthato.
rows.english: Instead of adopting any such misinterpretation, the difference should be treated as residing in the letter only, not in the meaning.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Natthi etesaṃ antaranti hi anantarā.
rows.english: There is no interval (antara) between them, thus they are proximate (anantara);

rows.pali: Saṇṭhānābhāvato suṭṭhu anantarāti samanantarā.
rows.english: they are quite without interval because [even the distinction of] co- presence is lacking, thus they are contiguous (samanantara). 14

rows.pali: 599.Uppajjamānova saha uppādanabhāvena upakārako dhammo sahajātapaccayo pakāsassa padīpo viya.
rows.english: 77. (6) A state that, while arising, assists [another state] by making it arise together with itself is a conascence condition, as a lamp is for illumination.

rows.pali: So arūpakkhandhādivasena chabbidho hoti.
rows.english: With the immaterial aggregates, etc., it is sixfold,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "cattāro khandhā arūpino aññamaññaṃ sahajātapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “(i) The four immaterial aggregates are a condition, as conascence condition, for each other, (ii) the four great primaries are … for each other; (iii) at the moment of descent into the womb mentality and materiality are … for each other; (iv) states of consciousness and its concomitants are … for the kinds of materiality originated by consciousness;

rows.pali: Cattāro mahābhūtā aññamaññaṃ, okkantikkhaṇe nāmarūpaṃ aññamaññaṃ, cittacetasikā dhammā cittasamuṭṭhānānaṃ rūpānaṃ, mahābhūtā upādārūpānaṃ, rūpino dhammā arūpīnaṃ dhammānaṃ kiñcikāle sahajātapaccayena paccayo, kiñcikāle na sahajātapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.6).
rows.english: (v) the great primaries are … for derived materiality; (vi) material states are sometimes [as at rebirth-linking] a condition, as conascence condition, and sometimes [as in the course of an existence] not a condition as conascence condition, for immaterial states” (Paṭṭh I 3).

rows.pali: Idaṃ hadayavatthumeva sandhāya vuttaṃ.
rows.english: This refers only to the heart-basis.

rows.pali: 600.Aññamaññaṃ uppādanupatthambhanabhāvena upakārako dhammo aññamaññapaccayo aññamaññūpatthambhakaṃ tidaṇḍakaṃ viya.
rows.english: 78.(7) A state that assists by means of mutual arousing and consolidating is a mutuality condition, as the three sticks of a tripod give each other consolidating support.

rows.pali: So arūpakkhandhādivasena tividho hoti.
rows.english: With the immaterial aggregates, etc., it is threefold,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "cattāro khandhā arūpino aññamaññapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “The four immaterial aggregates are a condition, as mutuality condition, [for each other];

rows.pali: Cattāro mahābhūtā okkantikkhaṇe nāmarūpaṃ aññamaññapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.7).
rows.english: the four great primaries are a condition, as mutuality condition, [for each other]; at the moment of descent into the womb mentality and materiality are a condition, as mutuality condition, [for each other]” (Paṭṭh I 3).

rows.pali: 601.Adhiṭṭhānākārena nissayākārena ca upakārako dhammo nissayapaccayo tarucittakammādīnaṃ pathavīpaṭādayo viya.
rows.english: 79.(8) A state that assists in the mode of foundation and in the mode of support is a support condition, as the earth is for trees, as canvas is for paintings, and so on.

rows.pali: So "cattāro khandhā arūpino aññamaññaṃ nissayapaccayena paccayo"ti evaṃ sahajāte vuttanayeneva veditabbo.
rows.english: It should be understood in the way stated for conascence thus: “The four immaterial aggregates are a condition, as support condition, for each other” (Paṭṭh I 3),

rows.pali: Chaṭṭho panettha koṭṭhāso "cakkhāyatanaṃ cakkhuviññāṇadhātuyā - pe - sota… ghāna… jivhā… kāyāyatanaṃ kāyaviññāṇadhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ nissayapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: but the sixth instance has been set forth in this way here: “The eye base [is a condition, as support condition,] for the eye-consciousness element [and for the states associated therewith]; the ear base … the nose base … the tongue base … the body base is a condition, as support condition, for the body- consciousness element and for the states associated therewith;

rows.pali: Yaṃ rūpaṃ nissāya manodhātu ca manoviññāṇadhātu ca vattanti, taṃ rūpaṃ manodhātuyā ca manoviññāṇadhātuyā ca taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ nissayapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.8) evaṃ vibhatto.
rows.english: the materiality with which as their support the mind element and the mind-consciousness element occur is a condition, as support condition, for the mind element, for the mind- consciousness element, and for the states associated therewith” (Paṭṭh I 4).

rows.pali: 602.Upanissayapaccayoti ettha pana ayaṃ tāva vacanattho, tadadhīnavuttitāya attano phalena nissito na paṭikkhittoti nissayo.
rows.english: 80. (9) Decisive-support condition: firstly, here is the word-meaning: [536] it is treated as support, not dispensed with, by its own fruit because [its own fruit’s] existence is dependent on it, thus it is the support.

rows.pali: Yathā pana bhuso āyāso upāyāso, evaṃ bhuso nissayo upanissayo, balavakāraṇassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: But just as great misery is despair, so great support is decisive support. This is a term for a cogent reason.

rows.pali: Tasmā balavakāraṇabhāvena upakārako dhammo upanissayapaccayoti veditabbo.
rows.english: Consequently, a state that assists by being a cogent reason should be understood as a decisive-support condition.

rows.pali: So ārammaṇūpanissayo anantarūpanissayo pakatūpanissayoti tividho hoti.
rows.english: It is threefold, namely, (a) object-decisive-support, (b) proximate-decisive- support, and (c) natural-decisive-support condition.

rows.pali: Tattha "dānaṃ datvā sīlaṃ samādiyitvā uposathakammaṃ katvā taṃ garuṃkatvā paccavekkhati, pubbe suciṇṇāni garuṃkatvā paccavekkhati, jhānā vuṭṭhahitvā jhānaṃ garuṃkatvā paccavekkhati, sekkhā gotrabhuṃ garuṃkatvā paccavekkhanti, vodānaṃ garuṃkatvā paccavekkhanti.
rows.english: 81. (a) Herein, firstly, “Having given a gift, having undertaken the precepts of virtue, having done the duties of the Uposatha, a man gives that importance and reviews it; he gives importance to former things well done and reviews them. Having emerged from jhāna, he gives jhāna importance and reviews it.

rows.pali: Sekkhā maggā vuṭṭhahitvā maggaṃ garuṃkatvā paccavekkhantī"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.423) evamādinā nayena ārammaṇūpanissayo tāva ārammaṇādhipatinā saddhiṃ nānattaṃ akatvāva vibhatto.
rows.english: Trainers give importance to change-of- lineage and review it. They give importance to cleansing and review it.15 Trainers, having emerged from a path, give importance to the path and review it” (Paṭṭh I 165). - object-decisive-support condition is set forth without differentiating it from object-predominance in this way.

rows.pali: Tattha yaṃ ārammaṇaṃ garuṃkatvā cittacetasikā uppajjanti, taṃ niyamato tesu ārammaṇesu balavārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Herein, the object in giving importance to which consciousness and consciousness concomitants arise, is necessarily a cogent one among these objects.

rows.pali: Iti garukattabbamattaṭṭhena ārammaṇādhipati, balavakāraṇaṭṭhena ārammaṇūpanissayoti evametesaṃ nānattaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So their difference may be understood in this way: object-predominance is in the sense of what is to be given importance to, and object-decisive-support is in the sense of a cogent reason.

rows.pali: Anantarūpanissayopi "purimā purimā kusalā khandhā pacchimānaṃ pacchimānaṃ kusalānaṃ khandhānaṃ upanissayapaccayena paccayo"tiādinā (paṭṭhā. 1.1.9) nayena anantarapaccayena saddhiṃ nānattaṃ akatvāva vibhatto.
rows.english: 82. (b) Also proximate-decisive-support condition is set forth without differentiating it from the proximity condition in the way beginning, “Any preceding profitable aggregates are a condition, as decisive-support condition, for any succeeding aggregates” (Paṭṭh I 165).

rows.pali: Mātikānikkhepe pana nesaṃ "cakkhuviññāṇadhātu taṃsampayuttakā ca dhammā manodhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ anantarapaccayena paccayo"tiādinā (paṭṭhā. 1.1.4) nayena anantarassa, "purimā purimā kusalā dhammā pacchimānaṃ pacchimānaṃ kusalānaṃ dhammānaṃ upanissayapaccayena paccayo"tiādinā (paṭṭhā. 1.1.9) nayena upanissayassa āgatattā nikkhepe viseso atthi.
rows.english: But in the exposition there is a distinction, because in the exposition of the schedule (mātikā-nikkhepa) they are given as proximity in the way beginning, “Eye-consciousness element and the states associated therewith are a condition, as proximity condition, for mind element and for the states associated therewith” (Paṭṭh I 2) and as decisive- support in the way beginning, “Any preceding profitable states are a condition as decisive-support condition, for any succeeding profitable states” (Paṭṭh I 4),

rows.pali: Sopi atthato ekībhāvameva gacchati.
rows.english: though it comes to the same thing as regards the meaning.

rows.pali: Evaṃ santepi attano attano anantarā anurūpassa cittuppādassa pavattanasamatthatāya anantaratā, purimacittassa pacchimacittuppādane balavatāya anantarūpanissayatā veditabbā.
rows.english: Nevertheless, proximity may be understood as the ability to cause the occurrence of an appropriate conscious arising proximate (next) to itself, and decisive support as the preceding consciousness’s cogency in the arousing of the succeeding consciousnesses.

rows.pali: Yathā hi hetupaccayādīsu kiñci dhammaṃ vināpi cittaṃ uppajjati, na evaṃ anantaracittaṃ vinā cittassa uppatti nāma atthi.
rows.english: 83. For while in the cases of root-cause and other such conditions consciousness can arise actually without any of those conditions, there is no arising of consciousness without a proximate consciousness [to precede it],

rows.pali: Tasmā balavapaccayo hoti.
rows.english: so this is a cogent condition.

rows.pali: Iti attano attano anantarā anurūpacittuppādanavasena anantarapaccayo, balavakāraṇavasena anantarūpanissayoti evametesaṃ nānattaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Their difference, then, may be understood in this way: proximity condition arouses an appropriate consciousness proximate (next) to itself, while proximity-decisive-support condition is a cogent reason.

rows.pali: Pakatūpanissayo pana pakato upanissayo pakatūpanissayo.
rows.english: 84.(c) As to natural-decisive-support: the decisive-support is natural, thus it is a natural-decisive-support.

rows.pali: Pakato nāma attano santāne nipphādito vā saddhāsīlādi upasevito vā utubhojanādi.
rows.english: Faith, virtue, etc., produced in, or climate, food, etc., habitual to, one’s own continuity are called natural.

rows.pali: Pakatiyā eva vā upanissayo pakatūpanissayo, ārammaṇānantarehi asammissoti attho.
rows.english: Or else, it is a decisive- support by nature, [537] thus it is a natural-decisive-support. The meaning is that it is unmixed with object and proximity.

rows.pali: Tassa pakatūpanissayo "saddhaṃ upanissāya dānaṃ deti, sīlaṃ samādiyati, uposathakammaṃ karoti, jhānaṃ uppādeti, vipassanaṃ uppādeti, maggaṃ uppādeti, abhiññaṃ uppādeti, samāpattiṃ uppādeti.
rows.english: It should be understood as variously divided up in the way beginning: “Natural-decisive-support: with faith as decisive-support a man gives a gift, undertakes the precepts of virtue, does the duties of the Uposatha, arouses jhāna, arouses insight, arouses the path, arouses direct-knowledge, arouses an attainment.

rows.pali: Sīlaṃ, sutaṃ, cāgaṃ, paññaṃ upanissāya dānaṃ deti - pe - samāpattiṃ uppādeti.
rows.english: With virtue … With learning … With generosity … With understanding as decisive-support a man gives a gift … arouses an attainment.

rows.pali: Saddhā, sīlaṃ, sutaṃ, cāgo, paññā saddhāya, sīlassa, sutassa, cāgassa, paññāya, upanissayapaccayena paccayo"tiādinā (paṭṭhā. 1.1.423) nayena anekappakārato pabhedo veditabbo.
rows.english: Faith, virtue, learning, generosity, understanding, are conditions, as decisive-support condition, for [the repeated arising of] faith, virtue, learning, generosity, understanding” (Paṭṭh I 165).

rows.pali: Iti ime saddhādayo pakatā ceva balavakāraṇaṭṭhena upanissayā cāti pakatūpanissayoti.
rows.english: So these things beginning with faith are natural-decisive-support since they are both natural and decisive-supports in the sense of a cogent reason.

rows.pali: 603.Paṭhamataraṃ uppajjitvā vattamānabhāvena upakārako dhammo purejātapaccayo.
rows.english: 85. (10) A state that assists by being present, having arisen previously, is a prenascence condition.

rows.pali: So pañcadvāre vatthārammaṇahadayavatthuvasena ekādasavidho hoti.
rows.english: It is elevenfold as physical basis and object in the five doors, and as the heart-basis,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "cakkhāyatanaṃ cakkhuviññāṇadhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ purejātapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “The eye base is a condition, as prenascence condition, for the eye-consciousness element and for the states associated therewith.

rows.pali: Sota - pe - ghāna, jivhā, kāyāyatanaṃ, rūpa, sadda, gandha, rasa, phoṭṭhabbāyatanaṃ kāyaviññāṇadhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ purejātapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: The ear base … The nose base … The tongue base … The body base … The visible-data base … The sound base … The odour base … The flavour base … The tangible-data base is a condition, as prenascence condition, for the body-consciousness element and for the states associated therewith.

rows.pali: Rūpa, sadda, gandha, rasa, phoṭṭhabbāyatanaṃ manodhātuyā.
rows.english: The visible-data base … The sound base … The odour base … The flavour base … the tangible data base [is a condition, as prenascence condition,] for the mind element.

rows.pali: Yaṃ rūpaṃ nissāya manodhātu ca manoviññāṇadhātu ca vattanti, taṃ rūpaṃ manodhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ purejātapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: The materiality with which as their support the mind element and mind- consciousness element occur is a condition, as prenascence condition,

rows.pali: Manoviññāṇadhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ kiñcikāle purejātapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: for the mind-element and for the states associated therewith, and it is sometimes [as in the course of an existence] a condition, as prenascence condition, for the mind- consciousness element and for the states associated therewith” (Paṭṭh I 4–5).

rows.pali: Kiñcikāle na purejātapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.10).
rows.english: sometimes [as at rebirth-linking] not a condition, as prenascence condition.

rows.pali: 604.Purejātānaṃ rūpadhammānaṃ upatthambhakattena upakārako arūpadhammo pacchājātapaccayo gijjhapotakasarīrānaṃ āhārāsācetanā viya.
rows.english: 86. (11) An immaterial state that [while present] assists prenascent material states [also present] by consolidating them is a postnascence condition, like the volition of appetite for food, which assists the bodies of vultures’ young.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "pacchājātā cittacetasikā dhammā purejātassa imassa kāyassa pacchājātapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.11).
rows.english: Hence it is said: “Postnascent [538] states of consciousness and its concomitants are a condition, as postnascence condition, for the prenascent [co-present] body” (Paṭṭh I 5).

rows.pali: 605.Āsevanaṭṭhena anantarānaṃ paguṇabalavabhāvāya upakārako dhammo āsevanapaccayo ganthādīsu purimapurimābhiyogo viya.
rows.english: 87.(12) A state that assists the efficiency and power of the proximate (next) in the sense of repetition is a repetition condition, like repeated application to books and so on.

rows.pali: So kusalākusalakiriyajavanavasena tividho hoti.
rows.english: It is threefold as profitable, unprofitable, and functional impulsion;

rows.pali: Yathāha – "purimā purimā kusalā dhammā pacchimānaṃ pacchimānaṃ kusalānaṃ dhammānaṃ āsevanapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: for it is said: “Preceding profitable states are a condition, as repetition condition, for succeeding profitable states … Preceding unprofitable …

rows.pali: Purimā purimā akusalā - pe - kiriyābyākatā dhammā pacchimānaṃ pacchimānaṃ kiriyābyākatānaṃ dhammānaṃ āsevanapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.12).
rows.english: Preceding functional indeterminate states are a condition, as repetition condition, for succeeding functional indeterminate states” (Paṭṭh I 5).

rows.pali: 606.Cittapayogasaṅkhātena kiriyabhāvena upakārako dhammo kammapaccayo.
rows.english: 88. (13) A state that assists by means of the action called intervening of consciousness is a kamma condition.

rows.pali: So nānakkhaṇikāya ceva kusalākusalacetanāya sahajātāya ca sabbāyapi cetanāya vasena duvidho hoti.
rows.english: It is twofold as (a) profitable and unprofitable volition acting from a different time, and (b) as all conascent volition (see Paṭṭh I 172),

rows.pali: Yathāha – "kusalākusalaṃ kammaṃ vipākānaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ kammapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “Profitable and unprofitable kamma is a condition, as kamma condition, for resultant aggregates and for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed.

rows.pali: Cetanā sampayuttakānaṃ dhammānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ kammapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.13).
rows.english: Conascent volition is a condition, as kamma condition, for associated states and for the kinds of materiality originated thereby” (Paṭṭh I 5).

rows.pali: 607.Nirussāhasantabhāvena nirussāhasantabhāvāya upakārako vipākadhammo vipākapaccayo.
rows.english: 89. (14) A resultant state that, by effortless quiet, assists effortless quiet [in other states] is a kamma-result condition.

rows.pali: So pavatte taṃsamuṭṭhānānaṃ, paṭisandhiyaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ, sabbattha ca sampayuttadhammānaṃ paccayo hoti.
rows.english: In the course of an existence it is a condition for states originated by it, and at rebirth-linking for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed, and in both cases for the associated states,

rows.pali: Yathāha –"vipākābyākato eko khandho tiṇṇannaṃ khandhānaṃ cittasamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ vipākapaccayena paccayo - pe - paṭisandhikkhaṇe vipākābyākato eko khandho tiṇṇannaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ.
rows.english: according as it is said: “One resultant indeterminate aggregate is a condition, as kamma-result condition, for three aggregates and for the kinds of materiality originated by consciousness … At the moment of rebirth-linking one resultant indeterminate aggregate [is a condition …] for three aggregates …

rows.pali: Tayo khandhā ekassa khandhassa.
rows.english: Three aggregates [are a condition …] for one aggregate …

rows.pali: Dve khandhā dvinnaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ vipākapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: Two aggregates are a condition, as kamma-result condition, for two aggregates and for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed.

rows.pali: Khandhā vatthussa vipākapaccayena paccayo"ti.
rows.english: Aggregates are a condition, as kamma- result condition, for the physical basis” (Paṭṭh I 173).

rows.pali: 608.Rūpārūpānaṃ upatthambhakaṭṭhena upakārakā cattāro āhārā āhārapaccayo.
rows.english: 90. (15) The four kinds of nutriment, which assist material and immaterial states by consolidating them, are nutriment conditions,

rows.pali: Yathāha –"kabaḷīkāro āhāro imassa kāyassa āhārapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “Physical nutriment is a condition, as nutriment condition, for this body.

rows.pali: Arūpino āhārā sampayuttakānaṃ dhammānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ āhārapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.15).
rows.english: Immaterial nutriments are conditions, as nutriment condition, for associated states and for the kinds of materiality originated by them” (Paṭṭh I 5).

rows.pali: Pañhāvāre pana "paṭisandhikkhaṇe vipākābyākatā āhārā sampayuttakānaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ āhārapaccayena paccayo"tipi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.429) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the Question Section it is said: “At the moment of rebirth-linking, resultant indeterminate nutriments are conditions, as nutriment condition, for aggregates associated therewith and for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed” (Paṭṭh I 174).

rows.pali: 609.Adhipatiyaṭṭhena upakārakā itthindriyapurisindriyavajjā vīsatindriyā indriyapaccayo.
rows.english: 91. (16) Leaving out the femininity and masculinity faculties, the twenty remaining faculties (see XIV.1), which assist in the sense of predominance, [539] are faculty conditions.

rows.pali: Tattha cakkhundriyādayo arūpadhammānaṃyeva, sesā rūpārūpānaṃ paccayā honti.
rows.english: Herein, the five, namely, the eye faculty, etc., are conditions only for immaterial states, the rest are conditions for material and immaterial states,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "cakkhundriyaṃ cakkhuviññāṇadhātuyā - pe - sota… ghāna… jivhā… kāyindriyaṃ kāyaviññāṇadhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ indriyapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “The eye faculty [is a condition, as faculty condition,] for eye-consciousness element [and for the states associated therewith]. The ear faculty … The nose faculty … The tongue faculty … The body faculty is a condition, as faculty condition, for the body-consciousness element and for the states associated therewith.

rows.pali: Rūpajīvitindriyaṃ kaṭattārūpānaṃ indriyapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: The material life faculty is a condition, as faculty condition, for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed.

rows.pali: Arūpino indriyā sampayuttakānaṃ dhammānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ indriyapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.16).
rows.english: The immaterial faculties are a condition, as faculty condition, for the states associated therewith and for the kinds of materiality originated thereby” (Paṭṭh 1, 5–6).

rows.pali: Pañhāvāre pana "paṭisandhikkhaṇe vipākābyākatā indriyā sampayuttakānaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ indriyapaccayena paccayo"tipi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.430) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the Question Section it is said: “At the moment of rebirth-linking resultant indeterminate faculties are a condition, as faculty condition, for associated aggregates and for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed” (Paṭṭh I 175).

rows.pali: 610.Upanijjhāyanaṭṭhena upakārakāni ṭhapetvā dvipañcaviññāṇe sukhadukkhavedanādvayaṃ sabbānipi kusalādibhedāni satta jhānaṅgāni jhānapaccayo.
rows.english: 92.(17) All the seven jhāna factors classed as profitable, etc.—leaving out the pair, pleasant and painful feeling, in the case of the two sets of five consciousnesses—which factors assist in the sense of constituting a state of jhāna, are jhāna conditions,

rows.pali: Yathāha –"jhānaṅgāni jhānasampayuttakānaṃ dhammānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ jhānapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.17).
rows.english: according as it is said: “The jhāna factors are a condition, as jhāna condition, for the states associated with jhāna and for the kinds of materiality originated thereby” (Paṭṭh I 6).

rows.pali: Pañhāvāre pana "paṭisandhikkhaṇe vipākābyākatāni jhānaṅgāni sampayuttakānaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ jhānapaccayena paccayo"tipi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.431) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the Question Section it is said: “At the moment of rebirth-linking, resultant indeterminate jhāna factors are a condition, as jhāna condition, for associated aggregates and for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed” (Paṭṭh I 175).

rows.pali: 611.Yato tato vā niyyānaṭṭhena upakārakāni kusalādibhedāni dvādasa maggaṅgāni maggapaccayo.
rows.english: 93.(18) The twelve path factors classed as profitable, etc., which assist in the sense of an outlet from whatever it may be, are path conditions,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "maggaṅgāni maggasampayuttakānaṃ dhammānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ maggapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.18).
rows.english: according as it is said: “The path factors are a condition, as path condition, for states associated therewith and for the kinds of materiality originated thereby” (Paṭṭh I 6).

rows.pali: Pañhāvāre pana "paṭisandhikkhaṇe vipākābyākatāni maggaṅgāni sampayuttakānaṃ khandhānaṃ kaṭattā ca rūpānaṃ maggapaccayena paccayo"tipi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.432) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the Question Section it is said: “At the moment of rebirth-linking, resultant indeterminate path factors are a condition, as path condition, for aggregates associated therewith and for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed” (Paṭṭh I 176).

rows.pali: Ete pana dvepi jhānamaggapaccayā dvipañcaviññāṇāhetukacittesu na labbhantīti veditabbā.
rows.english: But these two, namely, jhāna and path conditions, should be understood as inapplicable to the two sets of five consciousnesses and to the consciousnesses without root-cause ((34)–(41), (50)–(56), (70)–(72)).

rows.pali: 612.Ekavatthukaekārammaṇaekuppādekanirodhasaṅkhātena sampayuttabhāvena upakārakā arūpadhammā sampayuttapaccayo.
rows.english: 94. (19) Immaterial states that assist by the kind of association consisting in having the same physical basis, same object, same arising, same cessation, are association conditions,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "cattāro khandhā arūpino aññamaññaṃ sampayuttapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.19).
rows.english: according as it is said: “The four immaterial aggregates are a condition, as association condition, for each other” (Paṭṭh I 6).

rows.pali: 613.Ekavatthukādibhāvānupagamena upakārakā rūpino dhammā arūpīnaṃ dhammānaṃ, arūpinopi rūpīnaṃ vippayuttapaccayo.
rows.english: 95.(20) Material states that assist immaterial states, and immaterial states that assist material states by not having sameness of physical basis, etc., are dissociation conditions.

rows.pali: So sahajātapacchājātapurejātavasena tividho hoti.
rows.english: This is threefold as conascent, postnascent, and prenascent,

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "sahajātā kusalā khandhā cittasamuṭṭhānānaṃ rūpānaṃ vippayuttapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: for this is said: “Conascent profitable aggregates are a condition, as dissociation condition, for the kinds of materiality originated by consciousness.

rows.pali: Pacchājātā kusalā khandhā purejātassa imassa kāyassa vippayuttapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.434).
rows.english: Postnascent [540] profitable [mental] aggregates are a condition, as dissociation condition, for this prenascent body” (Paṭṭh I 176).

rows.pali: Abyākatapadassa pana sahajātavibhaṅge "paṭisandhikkhaṇe vipākābyākatā khandhā kaṭattārūpānaṃ vippayuttapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: But in the analysis of the conascent in the indeterminate clause it is said: “At the moment of rebirth-linking, resultant indeterminate aggregates are a condition, as dissociation condition, for the kinds of materiality due to kamma performed.

rows.pali: Khandhā vatthussa.
rows.english: The aggregates are a condition,

rows.pali: Vatthu khandhānaṃ vippayuttapaccayena paccayo"tipi (paṭṭhā. 1.1.434) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: as dissociation condition, for the physical basis, and the physical basis for the aggregates” (Paṭṭh I 176).

rows.pali: Purejātaṃ pana cakkhundriyādivatthuvaseneva veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: But the prenascent should be understood as the eye faculty, etc., and the physical basis,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "purejātaṃ cakkhāyatanaṃ cakkhuviññāṇassa - pe - kāyāyatanaṃ kāyaviññāṇassa vippayuttapaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: according as it is said: “The prenascent eye base [is a condition, as dissociation condition,] for eye-consciousness … The body base is a condition, as dissociation condition, for body-consciousness.

rows.pali: Vatthu vipākābyākatānaṃ kiriyābyākatānaṃ khandhānaṃ - pe - vatthu kusalānaṃ khandhānaṃ - pe - vatthu akusalānaṃ khandhānaṃ vippayuttapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.434).
rows.english: The physical basis [is a condition, as dissociation condition,] for resultant- indeterminate and functional-indeterminate aggregates … The physical basis [is a condition, as dissociation condition,] for profitable aggregates … The physical basis [is a condition, as dissociation condition,] for unprofitable aggregates” (Paṭṭh I 176–77).

rows.pali: 614.Paccuppannalakkhaṇena atthibhāvena tādisasseva dhammassa upatthambhakattena upakārako dhammo atthipaccayo.
rows.english: 96.(21) A state that, by means of existingness characterized by presence, assists a like state by consolidating it, is a presence condition.

rows.pali: Tassa arūpakkhandhamahābhūtanāmarūpacittacetasikamahābhūtaāyatanavatthuvasena sattadhā mātikā nikkhittā.
rows.english: A sevenfold summary is laid down for it according to immaterial aggregates, great primaries, mentality- materiality, consciousness and consciousness-concomitants, great primaries, bases, and physical [heart] basis,

rows.pali: Yathāha –"cattāro khandhā arūpino aññamaññaṃ atthipaccayena paccayo, cattāro mahābhūtā, okkantikkhaṇe nāmarūpaṃ aññamaññaṃ.
rows.english: according as it is said: “The four immaterial aggregates are a condition, as presence condition, for each other. The four great primaries … are … for each other. At the time of descent into the womb mentality and materiality [are a condition, as presence condition,] for each other.

rows.pali: Cittacetasikā dhammā cittasamuṭṭhānānaṃ rūpānaṃ.
rows.english: States of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants are … for the kinds of materiality originated by consciousness.

rows.pali: Mahābhūtā upādārūpānaṃ.
rows.english: The four great primaries are … for derived materiality.

rows.pali: Cakkhāyatanaṃ cakkhuviññāṇadhātuyā - pe - kāyāyatanaṃ - pe - rūpāyatanaṃ - pe - phoṭṭhabbāyatanaṃ kāyaviññāṇadhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ atthipaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: The eye base is … for the eye-consciousness element [and for the states associated therewith]. The [ear base … nose base … tongue base …] body base is … for the body-consciousness element … The visible-data base [is … for the eye-consciousness element … The sound base … odour base … flavour base …] tangible-data base is a condition, as presence condition, for the body- consciousness element and for the states associated therewith.

rows.pali: Rūpāyatanaṃ - pe - phoṭṭhabbāyatanaṃ manodhātuyā taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ.
rows.english: The visible-data base … The [sound base … odour base … flavour base …] tangible-data base is a condition, as presence condition, for the mind element and for the states associated therewith.

rows.pali: Yaṃ rūpaṃ nissāya manodhātu ca manoviññāṇadhātu ca vattanti, taṃ rūpaṃ manodhātuyā ca manoviññāṇadhātuyā ca taṃsampayuttakānañca dhammānaṃ atthipaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.21).
rows.english: The materiality with which as their support the mind element and mind-consciousness element occur is a condition, as presence condition, for the mind element, for the mind-consciousness element, and for the states associated therewith” (Paṭṭh I 6).

rows.pali: Pañhāvāre pana sahajātaṃ purejātaṃ pacchājātaṃ āhāraṃ indriyantipi nikkhipitvā sahajāte tāva "eko khandho tiṇṇannaṃ khandhānaṃ taṃsamuṭṭhānānañca rūpānaṃ atthipaccayena paccayo"tiādinā (paṭṭhā. 1.1.435) nayena niddeso kato, purejāte purejātānaṃ cakkhādīnaṃ vasena niddeso kato.
rows.english: 97. But in the Question Section, after setting forth conascence, prenascence, postnascence, nutriment, and faculty, the description is given first under conascence in the way beginning, “One aggregate is a condition, as presence condition, for three aggregates and for materiality originated thereby” (Paṭṭh I 178). Under prenascence the description is given according to the prenascent eye and so on.

rows.pali: Pacchājāte purejātassa imassa kāyassa pacchājātānaṃ cittacetasikānaṃ paccayavasena niddeso kato.
rows.english: Under postnascence the description is given according to postnascent consciousness and consciousness-concomitants as conditions for this body.

rows.pali: Āhārindriyesu "kabaḷīkāro āhāro imassa kāyassa atthipaccayena paccayo.
rows.english: Under nutriments and faculties [respectively] the description is given thus: “Physical nutriment is a condition, as presence condition, for this body,” [541]

rows.pali: Rūpajīvitindriyaṃ kaṭattārūpānaṃ atthipaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.435) evaṃ niddeso katoti.
rows.english: and “The material life faculty is a condition, as presence condition, for materiality due to kamma performed” (Paṭṭh I 178).16

rows.pali: 615.Attano anantarā uppajjamānānaṃ arūpadhammānaṃ pavattiokāsadānena upakārakā samanantaraniruddhā arūpadhammā natthipaccayo.
rows.english: 98.(22) Immaterial states that, by their ceasing in contiguity [before], assist by giving opportunity to immaterial states that arise proximately (next) after them are absence conditions,

rows.pali: Yathāha –"samanantaraniruddhā cittacetasikā dhammā paṭuppannānaṃ cittacetasikānaṃ dhammānaṃ natthipaccayena paccayo"ti.
rows.english: according as it is said: “States of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants that have ceased in contiguity are a condition, as absence condition, for present states of consciousness and consciousness- concomitants” (Paṭṭh I 7).

rows.pali: Te eva vigatabhāvena upakārakattā vigatapaccayo.
rows.english: 99. (23) Those same states, because they assist by their disappearance, are a disappearance condition,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "samanantaravigatā cittacetasikā dhammā paṭuppannānaṃ cittacetasikānaṃ dhammānaṃ vigatapaccayena paccayo"ti.
rows.english: according as it is said: “States of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants that have disappeared in contiguity are a condition, as disappearance condition, for present states of consciousness and consciousness-concomitants” (Paṭṭh I 7).

rows.pali: Atthi paccayadhammā eva ca avigatabhāvena upakārakattā avigatapaccayoti veditabbā.
rows.english: 100. (24) The same states that are presence condition, because they assist by their non-disappearance, should be understood as a non-disappearance condition.

rows.pali: Desanāvilāsena pana tathā vinetabbaveneyyavasena vā ayaṃ duko vutto, ahetukadukaṃ vatvāpi hetuvippayuttaduko viyāti.
rows.english: Or this dyad is stated as an embellishment of teaching to suit the needs of those who are teachable, just as [in the Mātikā of the Dhammasaṅgaṇī] the “dissociated- from-cause dyad” is given after the “causeless dyad.”

rows.pali: 616.Evamimesu catuvīsatiyā paccayesu ayaṃ avijjā,
rows.english: 101. Now, as regards these twenty-four conditions: ignorance

rows.pali: Tattha puññānaṃ duvidhāti ārammaṇapaccayena ca upanissayapaccayena cāti dvedhā paccayo hoti.
rows.english: 102. Herein, for those of merit ignorance is a condition in two ways: it is a condition in two ways, namely, as object condition and as decisive-support condition.

rows.pali: Sā hi avijjaṃ khayato vayato sammasanakāle kāmāvacarānaṃ puññābhisaṅkhārānaṃ ārammaṇapaccayena paccayo hoti.
rows.english: For ignorance is a condition, as object condition, for formations of merit of the sense sphere at the time of comprehending [by means of insight] ignorance as liable to destruction and fall;

rows.pali: Abhiññācittena samohacittaṃ jānanakāle rūpāvacarānaṃ.
rows.english: and it is likewise for those of the fine-material sphere at the time of knowing a confused mind by means of direct-knowledge consciousness [through penetrating others’ minds, and so on].

rows.pali: Avijjāsamatikkamatthāya pana dānādīni ceva kāmāvacarapuññakiriyavatthūni pūrentassa, rūpāvacarajjhānāni ca uppādentassa dvinnampi tesaṃ upanissayapaccayena paccayo hoti.
rows.english: But it is a condition, as decisive- support condition, in two cases, that is to say, [for the sense-sphere formation] in one who, for the purpose of surmounting ignorance, fulfils the various instances of sense-sphere merit-making consisting in giving, etc., and [for the fine-material- sphere formation] in one who arouses the fine-material jhānas [for the same purpose].

rows.pali: Tathā avijjāsammūḷhattā kāmabhavarūpabhavasampattiyo patthetvā tāneva puññāni karontassa.
rows.english: Likewise in one who effects that merit while aspiring to the delight of sense-sphere becoming and fine-material becoming because he is confused by ignorance.

rows.pali: Anekadhā pana paresanti apuññābhisaṅkhārānaṃ anekadhā paccayo hoti.
rows.english: 103. And for the next in many ways: it is a condition for formations of demerit in many ways.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Esā hi avijjaṃ ārabbha rāgādīnaṃ uppajjanakāle ārammaṇapaccayena, garuṃkatvā assādanakāle ārammaṇādhipatiārammaṇūpanissayehi, avijjāsammūḷhassa anādīnavadassāvino pāṇātipātādīni karontassa upanissayapaccayena, dutiyajavanādīnaṃ anantarasamanantaraanantarūpanissayāsevananatthivigatapaccayehi, yaṃkiñci akusalaṃ karontassa hetu sahajāta aññamañña nissaya sampayutta atthi avigatapaccayehīti anekadhā paccayo hoti.
rows.english: As object condition at the time of the arising of greed, etc., contingent upon ignorance; as object-predominance and object-decisive-support respectively at the times of giving importance [to ignorance] and enjoying [it]; as decisive-support in one who, being confused by ignorance and unaware of danger, kills living things, etc.; as proximity, contiguity, proximity-decisive- support, repetition, absence, and disappearance, for the second impulsion and those that follow; as root-cause, conascence, mutuality, support, association, presence, and non-disappearance, in one doing anything unprofitable. It is thus a condition in many ways.

rows.pali: Pacchimānaṃ sā ekadhā paccayo matāti āneñjābhisaṅkhārānaṃ upanissayapaccayeneva ekadhā paccayo matā.
rows.english: 104. But for the last kind only once: [542] it is reckoned as a condition in one way, namely, as decisive-support condition only, for formations of the imperturbable.

rows.pali: So panassā upanissayabhāvo puññābhisaṅkhāre vuttanayeneva veditabboti.
rows.english: But its relation as decisive-support condition should be understood as stated under formations of merit.

rows.pali: 617.Etthāha – kiṃ panāyamekāva avijjā saṅkhārānaṃ paccayo, udāhu aññepi paccayā santīti?
rows.english: 105.Here it may be asked: But how is this? Is ignorance the only condition for formations, or are there other conditions?

rows.pali: Kiṃ panettha, yadi tāva ekāva, ekakāraṇavādo āpajjati.
rows.english: What is the position here? For firstly, if it is the only one, there follows the assertion of a single cause;17

rows.pali: Athaññepi santi, "avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā"ti ekakāraṇaniddeso nupapajjatīti?
rows.english: but then if there are others, the description of it as a single cause, namely, “With ignorance as condition there are formations,” is incorrect—

rows.pali: Na nupapajjati.
rows.english: It is not incorrect.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why not?

rows.pali: Yasmā –
rows.english: Here is the reason:

rows.pali: Ekato hi kāraṇato na idha kiñci ekaṃ phalamatthi, na anekaṃ.
rows.english: 106. Here there is no single or multiple fruit of any kind from a single cause,

rows.pali: Nāpi anekehi kāraṇehi ekaṃ.
rows.english: nor a single fruit from multiple causes,

rows.pali: Anekehi pana kāraṇehi anekameva hoti.
rows.english: but only multiple fruit from multiple causes.

rows.pali: Tathā hi anekehi utupathavībījasalilasaṅkhātehi kāraṇehi anekameva rūpagandharasādikaṃ aṅkurasaṅkhātaṃ phalaṃ uppajjamānaṃ dissati.
rows.english: So from multiple causes, in other words, from temperature, earth, seed, and moisture, is seen to arise a multiple fruit, in other words, the shoot, which has visible form, odour, taste, and so on.

rows.pali: Yaṃ panetaṃ "avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā, saṅkhārapaccayā viññāṇa"nti ekekahetuphaladīpanaṃ kataṃ, tattha attho atthi, payojanaṃ vijjati.
rows.english: But one representative cause and fruit given in this way, “With ignorance as condition there are formations; with formations as condition, consciousness,” have a meaning and a use.

rows.pali: Bhagavā hi katthaci padhānattā, katthaci pākaṭattā, katthaci asādhāraṇattā desanāvilāsassa ca veneyyānañca anurūpato ekameva hetuṃ vā phalaṃ vā dīpeti.
rows.english: 107. For the Blessed One employs one representative cause and fruit when it is suitable for the sake of elegance in instruction and to suit the idiosyncrasies of those susceptible of being taught. And he does so in some instances because it is a basic factor, and in some instances because it is the most obvious, and in some instances because it is not common to all.

rows.pali: "Phassapaccayā vedanā"ti hi padhānattā ekameva hetuphalamāha.
rows.english: In the passage “With contact as condition, feeling” (M I 261) he mentions a single cause and fruit because they are basic factors.

rows.pali: Phasso hi vedanāya padhānahetu yathāphassaṃ vedanā vavatthānato.
rows.english: For contact is the basic cause of feeling since the kinds of feeling are defined according to the kinds of contact [as “eye-contact-born feeling” and so on],

rows.pali: Vedanā ca phassassa padhānaphalaṃ yathāvedanaṃ phassavavatthānato.
rows.english: and feeling is contact’s basic fruit since contact is defined according to the kinds of feeling [that it produces].

rows.pali: "Semhasamuṭṭhānā ābādhā"ti (a. ni. 10.60) pākaṭattā ekaṃ hetumāha.
rows.english: He mentions a single cause in the passage “Disease due to phlegm” (A V 110) because that is the most obvious.

rows.pali: Pākaṭo hi ettha semho, na kammādayo.
rows.english: For here what is obvious is the phlegm, not the kamma, etc., [mentioned later in the same sutta].

rows.pali: "Ye keci, bhikkhave, akusalā dhammā, sabbe te ayonisomanasikāramūlakā"ti asādhāraṇattā ekaṃ hetumāha.
rows.english: He mentions a single cause in the passage “Bhikkhus, any states whatever that are unprofitable are all rooted in unwise attention” (cf. S V 91) because it is not common to all.

rows.pali: Asādhāraṇo hi ayonisomanasikāro akusalānaṃ, sādhāraṇāni vatthārammaṇādīnīti.
rows.english: For unwise attention to unprofitable things is not common to all [states] in the way that, say, physical basis and object are common to all.

rows.pali: Tasmā ayamidha avijjā vijjamānesupi aññesu vatthārammaṇasahajātadhammādīsu saṅkhārakāraṇesu "assādānupassino taṇhā pavaḍḍhatī"ti (saṃ. ni. 2.52) ca "avijjāsamudayā āsavasamudayo"ti (ma. ni. 1.104) ca vacanato aññesampi taṇhādīnaṃ saṅkhārahetūnaṃ hetūti padhānattā, "avidvā, bhikkhave, avijjāgato puññābhisaṅkhārampi abhisaṅkharotī"ti pākaṭattā, asādhāraṇattā ca saṅkhārānaṃ hetubhāvena dīpitāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 108. Consequently, although other causes of formations such as physical basis and object, conascent states, etc., are actually existent, still ignorance may be understood as the representative cause of formations [firstly] because it is the basic factor as the cause of other causes of formations such as craving, etc., as it is said: “Craving increases in one who dwells seeing enjoyment” (S II 84), and “With the arising of ignorance there is the arising of cankers” (M I 55); and again because it is the most obvious, “Not knowing, bhikkhus, in ignorance, he forms the formation of merit” (cf. S II 82); and lastly because it is not common to all.

rows.pali: Eteneva ca ekekahetuphaladīpanaparihāravacanena sabbattha ekekahetuphaladīpane payojanaṃ veditabbanti.
rows.english: So the use of one representative cause and fruit should in each instance be understood according to this explanation of it. 18

rows.pali: 618.Etthāha – evaṃ santepi ekantāniṭṭhaphalāya sāvajjāya avijjāya kathaṃ puññāneñjābhisaṅkhārapaccayattaṃ yujjati?
rows.english: 109. Here it may be said: “We admit that. But ignorance is reprehensible and has entirely undesirable fruit. How then can it rightly be a condition for formations of merit and of the imperturbable?

rows.pali: Na hi nimbabījato ucchu uppajjatīti.
rows.english: Sugarcane does not grow from [bitter] nimba seeds.”

rows.pali: Kathaṃ na yujjissati?
rows.english: Why should it not be right?

rows.pali: Lokasmiñhi –
rows.english: For in the world [that is, even among thinkers outside the Dispensation it is recognized that]

rows.pali: Dhammānaṃ hi ṭhānasabhāvakiccādiviruddho cāviruddho ca paccayo loke siddho.
rows.english: 110. It is established in the world that when states have a condition, it may be opposed or unopposed to them as to presence, individual essence, function, and so on.

rows.pali: Purimacittaṃ hi aparacittassa ṭhānaviruddho paccayo, purimasippādisikkhā ca pacchā pavattamānānaṃ sippādikiriyānaṃ.
rows.english: For a preceding consciousness is a condition, opposed as to presence, for the succeeding consciousness; and the preceding training is a condition likewise for the plying of crafts, etc., which take place subsequently.

rows.pali: Kammaṃ rūpassa sabhāvaviruddho paccayo, khīrādīni ca dadhiādīnaṃ.
rows.english: Kamma is a condition, opposed as to individual essence, for materiality; and so are milk, etc., for curds, and so on.

rows.pali: Āloko cakkhuviññāṇassa kiccaviruddho, guḷādayo ca āsavādīnaṃ.
rows.english: Light is a condition, opposed as to function, for eye-consciousness; and so are molasses, etc., for intoxicants, and so on.

rows.pali: Cakkhurūpādayo pana cakkhuviññāṇādīnaṃ ṭhānāviruddhā paccayā.
rows.english: But eye-cum-visible-data, etc., are respectively a condition, unopposed as to presence, for eye-consciousness, and so on.

rows.pali: Purimajavanādayo pacchimajavanādīnaṃ sabhāvāviruddhā kiccāviruddhā ca.
rows.english: And the first impulsion, and those that follow, are a condition, unopposed as to individual essence and function, for the impulsions that follow them.

rows.pali: Yathā ca viruddhāviruddhā paccayā siddhā, evaṃ sadisāsadisāpi.
rows.english: And just as conditions operate as opposed and unopposed, so also they operate as like and unlike.

rows.pali: Sadisameva hi utuāhārasaṅkhātaṃ rūpaṃ rūpassa paccayo, sālibījādīni ca sāliphalādīnaṃ.
rows.english: Materiality—for example, temperature and nutriment—is a condition for materiality: the like for the like. And so are paddy seeds, etc., for paddy crops, and so on.

rows.pali: Asadisampi rūpaṃ arūpassa, arūpañca rūpassa paccayo hoti, golomāviloma-visāṇa-dadhitilapiṭṭhādīni ca dubbā-sarabhūtiṇakādīnaṃ.
rows.english: The material is a condition for the immaterial, and so is the immaterial for the material: the unlike for the like. And so are ox hair and ram’s hair, horns, curd, and sesame flour, etc., respectively for dabba grass, reeds, bhūtanaka grass, and so on. 19

rows.pali: Yesañca dhammānaṃ te viruddhāviruddhasadisāsadisapaccayā, na te dhammā tesaṃ dhammānaṃ vipākā eva.
rows.english: And those states for which these are the opposed and unopposed, like and unlike, conditions are not the results of these states as well.

rows.pali: Iti ayaṃ avijjā vipākavasena ekantāniṭṭhaphalā, sabhāvavasena ca sāvajjāpi samānā sabbesampi etesaṃ puññābhisaṅkhārādīnaṃ yathānurūpaṃ ṭhānakiccasabhāvaviruddhāviruddhapaccayavasena, sadisāsadisapaccayavasena ca paccayo hotīti veditabbā.
rows.english: 111. So although this ignorance has entirely undesirable fruit for its result and is reprehensible in its individual essence, yet it should be understood as a condition, opposed or unopposed and like or unlike as the case may be, as to presence, function, and individual essence, for all these formations of merit, etc.

rows.pali: So cassā paccayabhāvo "yassa hi dukkhādīsu avijjāsaṅkhātaṃ aññāṇaṃ appahīnaṃ hoti, so dukkhe tāva pubbantādīsu ca aññāṇena saṃsāradukkhaṃ sukhasaññāya gahetvā tassa hetubhūte tividhepi saṅkhāre ārabhatī"tiādinā nayena vutto eva.
rows.english: And its state as a condition has already been given in the way beginning, “For when unknowing—in other words, ignorance—of suffering, etc., is unaban- doned in a man, owing firstly to his unknowing about suffering and about the past, etc., then be believes the suffering of the round of rebirths to be pleasant and he embarks upon the three kinds of formations, which are the cause of that very suffering” (§62).

rows.pali: 619.Apica ayaṃ aññopi pariyāyo –
rows.english: 112. Moreover, there is this way of explanation as well:

rows.pali: Kathaṃ pana yo etesu vimuyhati, so tividhepete saṅkhāre karotīti ce.
rows.english: 113. But how does a man who is confused about these things perform these three kinds of formations?

rows.pali: Cutiyā tāva vimūḷho "sabbattha khandhānaṃ bhedo maraṇa"nti cutiṃ agaṇhanto "satto marati, sattassa dehantarasaṅkamana"ntiādīni vikappeti.
rows.english: Firstly, when he is confused about death, instead of taking death thus, “Death in every case is break-up of aggregates,” he figures that it is a [lasting] being that dies, that it is a [lasting] being’s transmigration to another incarnation, and so on.

rows.pali: Upapāte vimūḷho "sabbattha khandhānaṃ pātubhāvo jātī"ti upapātaṃ agaṇhanto "satto upapajjati, sattassa navasarīrapātubhāvo"tiādīni vikappeti.
rows.english: 114. When he is confused about reappearance, instead of taking rebirth thus, “Birth in every case is manifestation of aggregates,” he figures that it is a lasting being’s manifestation in a new body.

rows.pali: Saṃsāre vimūḷho yo esa,
rows.english: 115. when he is confused about the round of rebirths,

rows.pali: Evaṃ vaṇṇito saṃsāro, taṃ evaṃ agaṇhanto "ayaṃ satto asmā lokā paraṃ lokaṃ gacchati, parasmā lokā imaṃ lokaṃ āgacchatī"tiādīni vikappeti.
rows.english: instead of taking the round of rebirths as pictured thus, he figures that it is a lasting being that goes from this world to another world, that comes from another world to this world.

rows.pali: Saṅkhārānaṃ lakkhaṇe vimūḷho saṅkhārānaṃ sabhāvalakkhaṇaṃ sāmaññalakkhaṇañca agaṇhanto saṅkhāre attato attaniyato dhuvato sukhato subhato vikappeti.
rows.english: 116. When he is confused about the characteristics of formations, instead of apprehending their specific and general characteristics, he figures that formations are self, belong to a self, are lasting, pleasant, beautiful.

rows.pali: Paṭiccasamuppannadhammesu vimūḷho avijjādīhi saṅkhārādīnaṃ pavattiṃ agaṇhanto "attā jānāti vā na jānāti vā, so eva karoti ca kāreti ca.
rows.english: 117. When he is confused about dependently-arisen states, instead of taking the occurrence of formations to be due to ignorance, etc., he figures that it is a self that knows or does not know, that acts and causes action,

rows.pali: So paṭisandhiyaṃ upapajjati, tassa aṇuissarādayo kalalādibhāvena sarīraṃ saṇṭhapento indriyāni sampādenti.
rows.english: that appears in rebirth- linking, and he figures that atoms, an Overlord, etc., shape its body in the various states of the embryo and endow it with faculties,

rows.pali: So indriyasampanno phusati, vediyati, taṇhīyati, upādiyati, ghaṭiyati.
rows.english: and that when it has been endowed with faculties it touches, feels, craves, clings, and endeavours,

rows.pali: So puna bhavantare bhavatī"ti vā, "sabbe sattā niyatisaṅgatibhāvapariṇatā"ti (dī. ni. 1.168) vā vikappeti.
rows.english: and that it becomes anew in the next becoming; or he figures thus, “All beings … [are] moulded by fate, coincidence and nature” (D I 53).

rows.pali: So avijjāya andhīkato evaṃ vikappento yathā nāma andho pathaviyaṃ vicaranto maggampi amaggampi thalampi ninnampi samampi visamampi paṭipajjati, evaṃ puññampi apuññampi āneñjābhisaṅkhārampi abhisaṅkharotīti.
rows.english: 118. Thus he figures, blinded by ignorance. He is like a blind man who wanders about the earth, encountering now right and now wrong paths, now heights and now hollows, now even and now uneven ground, and so he forms formations now of merit, now of demerit, now imperturbable.

rows.pali: Tenetaṃ vuccati –
rows.english: 119. Hence this is said:

rows.pali: 620.Saṅkhārapaccayā viññāṇapade – viññāṇanti cakkhuviññāṇādi chabbidhaṃ.
rows.english: 120. In the clause, with formations as condition, consciousness, consciousness is sixfold as eye-consciousness, and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha cakkhuviññāṇaṃ kusalavipākaṃ akusalavipākanti duvidhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Herein, eye-consciousness is twofold, namely, profitable [kamma-]resultant and unprofitable [kamma-]resultant (see Table II for bracketed numbers that follow).

rows.pali: Tathā sotaghānajivhākaāyaviññāṇāni.
rows.english: Likewise ear-, nose-, tongue-, and body-consciousness ((34)–(38) and (50)–(54)).

rows.pali: Manoviññāṇaṃ kusalākusalavipākā dve manodhātuyo, tisso ahetukamanoviññāṇadhātuyo, aṭṭha sahetukāni kāmāvacaravipākacittāni, pañca rūpāvacarāni, cattāri arūpāvacarānīti bāvīsatividhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But mind-consciousness is twenty- two-fold, namely, the two profitable and unprofitable resultant mind elements ((39) and (55)), the three root-causeless mind-consciousness elements ((40), (41) and (56)), the eight sense-sphere resultant consciousnesses with root-cause ((42)–(49)), the five of the fine-material sphere ((57)–(61)), and the four of the immaterial sphere ((62)–(65)).

rows.pali: Iti imehi chahi viññāṇehi sabbānipi bāttiṃsa lokiyavipākaviññāṇāni saṅgahitāni honti.
rows.english: So all the thirty-two mundane resultant consciousnesses ((34)–(65)) are included by these six kinds of consciousness.

rows.pali: Lokuttarāni pana vaṭṭakathāya na yujjantīti na gahitāni.
rows.english: But the supramundane kinds do not belong to the exposition of the round [of becoming], and so they are not included.

rows.pali: Tattha siyā "kathaṃ panetaṃ jānitabbaṃ idaṃ vuttappakāraṃ viññāṇaṃ saṅkhārapaccayā hotī"ti?
rows.english: 121. Here it may be asked: “But how is it to be known that this consciousness of the kind stated actually has formations as its condition?”

rows.pali: Upacitakammābhāve vipākābhāvato.
rows.english: —Because there is no kamma-result when there is no stored-up kamma.

rows.pali: Vipākaṃ hetaṃ, vipākañca na upacitakammābhāve uppajjati.
rows.english: For this consciousness is kamma-result, and kamma-result does not arise in the absence of stored-up kamma.

rows.pali: Yadi uppajjeyya sabbesaṃ sabbavipākāni uppajjeyyuṃ, na ca uppajjantīti jānitabbametaṃ saṅkhārapaccayā idaṃ viññāṇaṃ hotīti.
rows.english: If it did, then all kinds of kamma-resultant consciousnesses would arise in all kinds of beings, and they do not do so. This is how it should be known that such consciousness has formations as its condition.

rows.pali: Katarasaṅkhārapaccayā kataraṃ viññāṇanti ce.
rows.english: 122. But which kind of consciousness has which kind of formations as its condition?

rows.pali: Kāmāvacarapuññābhisaṅkhārapaccayā tāva kusalavipākāni pañca cakkhuviññāṇādīni, manoviññāṇe ekā manodhātu, dve manoviññāṇadhātuyo, aṭṭha kāmāvacaramahāvipākānīti soḷasa.
rows.english: Firstly, the following sixteen kinds arise with the sense-sphere formation of merit as condition: the five profitable resultants beginning with eye- consciousness ((34)–(38)), and in the case of mind-consciousness one kind of mind element (39) and two kinds of mind-consciousness element ((40)–(41)), and the eight kinds of sense-sphere resultant ((42)–(49)),

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarapuññābhisaṅkhārapaccayā pana pañca rūpāvacaravipākāni.
rows.english: 123. There are five kinds of resultant fine-material-sphere consciousness ((57)– (61)) with the fine-material-sphere formation of merit as condition,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Apuññābhisaṅkhārapaccayāpana akusalavipākāni pañca cakkhuviññāṇādīni, ekā manodhātu, ekā manoviññāṇadhātūti evaṃ sattavidhaṃ viññāṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: the five unprofitable resultants beginning with eye-consciousness ((50)–(54)), one mind element (55), and one mind-consciousness element (56),

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Āneñjābhisaṅkhārapaccayā pana cattāri arūpavipākānīti evaṃ catubbidhaṃ viññāṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 125.There are four kinds of immaterial resultant consciousness ((62)–(65)) with the formation of the imperturbable as condition,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: 621.Evaṃ yaṃ saṅkhārapaccayā yaṃ viññāṇaṃ hoti, taṃ ñatvā idānissa evaṃ pavatti veditabbā – sabbameva hi idaṃ pavattipaṭisandhivasena dvedhā pavattati.
rows.english: 126. After knowing what kind of consciousness has what formations as its condition, one should now understand how it occurs as follows. Now, this resultant consciousness all occurs in two ways, namely, (a) in the course of an individual existence (or continuity), and (b) at the rebirth-linking [moment].

rows.pali: Tattha dve pañcaviññāṇāni, dve manodhātuyo, somanassasahagatā ahetukamanoviññāṇadhātūti imāni terasa pañcavokārabhave pavattiyaññeva pavattanti.
rows.english: Herein, there are the two fivefold consciousnesses ((34)–(38) and (50)–(54)), two mind elements ((39) and (55)), and root-causeless mind-consciousness element accompanied by joy (40), that is, thirteen which occur only in the course of an existence in the five-constituent kind of becoming.20

rows.pali: Sesāni ekūnavīsati tīsu bhavesu yathānurūpaṃ pavattiyampi paṭisandhiyampi pavattanti.
rows.english: The remaining nineteen occur in the three kinds of becoming, either in the course of an existence or at rebirth-linking, as appropriate.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Kusalavipākāni tāva cakkhuviññāṇādīni pañca kusalavipākena akusalavipākena vā nibbattassa yathākkamaṃ paripākaṃ upagatindriyassa cakkhādīnaṃ āpāthagataṃ iṭṭhaṃ iṭṭhamajjhattaṃ vā rūpādiārammaṇaṃ ārabbha cakkhādipasādaṃ nissāya dassanasavanaghāyanasāyanaphusanakiccaṃ sādhayamānāni pavattanti.
rows.english: 127. Firstly, in one who has been reborn by means of either profitable-result or unprofitable result: according as his faculties mature, so the five profitable- resultant eye-, etc., consciousnesses occur accomplishing the respective functions of seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, and touching ((d)–(h)), contingent respectively upon a desirable or desirable-neutral visible datum, etc., as object that has come into the focus of the eye, etc., and having the sensitivity of the eye, etc., as [material] support.

rows.pali: Tathā akusalavipākāni pañca.
rows.english: And likewise the five unprofitable-resultant consciousnesses;

rows.pali: Kevalañhi tesaṃ aniṭṭhaṃ aniṭṭhamajjhattaṃ vā ārammaṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: that the visible data, etc., as object for these are undesirable or undesirable-neutral.

rows.pali: Ayameva viseso.
rows.english: - the only difference being this.

rows.pali: Dasapi cetāni niyatadvārārammaṇavatthuṭṭhānāni niyatakiccāneva ca bhavanti.
rows.english: And these ten are invariable as to their door, object, physical basis, and position [in the cognitive series], and invariable as to their functions.

rows.pali: Tato kusalavipākānaṃ cakkhuviññāṇādīnaṃ anantarā kusalavipākā manodhātu tesaṃyeva ārammaṇaṃ ārabbha hadayavatthuṃ nissāya sampaṭicchanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā pavattati.
rows.english: 128. After that, next to the profitable-resultant eye-, etc., consciousness, the profitable-resultant mind element (39) occurs accomplishing the function of receiving (i), contingent upon the same object as that of the former, and having the heart-basis as support. And next after the unprofitable-resultant eye-, etc., consciousness,

rows.pali: Tathā akusalavipākānaṃ anantarā akusalavipākā.
rows.english: the unprofitable-resultant mind element (55) occurs likewise.

rows.pali: Idañca pana dvayaṃ aniyatadvārārammaṇaṃ niyatavatthuṭṭhānaṃ niyatakiccañca hoti.
rows.english: But these two, while variable as to door and object, are invariable as to physical basis and position, and invariable as to function.

rows.pali: Somanassasahagatā pana ahetukamanoviññāṇadhātu kusalavipākamanodhātuyā anantarā tassā eva ārammaṇaṃ ārabbha hadayavatthuṃ nissāya santīraṇakiccaṃ sādhayamānā chasu dvāresu balavārammaṇe kāmāvacarasattānaṃ yebhuyyena lobhasampayuttajavanāvasāne bhavaṅgavīthiṃ pacchinditvā javanena gahitārammaṇe tadārammaṇavasena ca sakiṃ vā dvikkhattuṃ vā pavattatīti majjhimaṭṭhakathāyaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 129. Then next to the profitable-resultant mind element, the root-causeless mind-consciousness element accompanied by joy (40) occurs accomplishing the function of investigation (j), contingent upon the same object as that of the mind element, and having the heart-basis as support. And when the object is a vivid one in any of the six doors belonging to sense-sphere beings, usually at the end of impulsions associated with greed it holds up the [renewal of the] course of the life-continuum (b) by occurring either once or twice as registration (m), having the same object as that apprehended by the impulsions—so it is said in the Majjhima Commentary.21

rows.pali: Abhidhammaṭṭhakathāyaṃ pana tadārammaṇe dve cittavārā āgatā.
rows.english: But in the Abhidhamma Commentary two turns of consciousness have been handed down with respect to registration.

rows.pali: Idaṃ pana cittaṃ tadārammaṇanti ca piṭṭhibhavaṅganti cāti dve nāmāni labhati.
rows.english: This consciousness has two names, “registration” (tad-ārammaṇa—lit. having that object that the preceding impulsions had) and “aftermath life-continuum” (piṭṭhi- bhavaṅga—see XIV.122).

rows.pali: Aniyatadvārārammaṇaṃ niyatavatthukaṃ aniyataṭṭhānakiccañca hotīti.
rows.english: It is variable as to door and object, it is invariable as to physical basis, and it is variable as to position and function.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva terasa pañcavokārabhave pavattiyaññeva pavattantīti veditabbāni.
rows.english: This, in the first place, it should be understood, is how thirteen kinds of consciousness occur only in the course of an individual existence in the five- constituent kind of becoming.

rows.pali: Sesesu ekūnavīsatiyā na kiñci attano anurūpāya paṭisandhiyā na pavattati.
rows.english: 130. As to the remaining nineteen ((41)–(49) and (56)–(65)), there is none that does not occur as a rebirth-linking (a) appropriate to it (see §133).

rows.pali: Pavattiyaṃ pana kusalākusalavipākā tāva dve ahetukamanoviññāṇadhātuyo pañcadvāre kusalākusalavipākamanodhātūnaṃ anantarā santīraṇakiccaṃ, chasu dvāresu pubbe vuttanayeneva tadārammaṇakiccaṃ, attanā dinnapaṭisandhito uddhaṃ asati bhavaṅgupacchedake cittuppāde bhavaṅgakiccaṃ, ante cutikiccañcāti cattāri kiccāni sādhayamānā niyatavatthukā aniyatadvārārammaṇaṭṭhānakiccā hutvā pavattanti.
rows.english: But in the course of an individual existence, firstly, two, namely, profitable-resultant and unprofitable-resultant root-causeless mind-consciousness elements ((41) and (56)) occur accomplishing four functions, that is to say, the function of investigating in the five doors (j) next after profitable-resultant and unprofitable-resultant mind element, the function of registration (m) in the six doors in the way already stated, the function of life-continuum (b) that continues after rebirth-linking given by themselves, as long as there is no thought-arising to interrupt the life- continuum, and lastly the function of death (n) at the end [of the course of an existence]. And so these two are invariable as to [possession of heart-] basis, and variable as to door, object, position, and function.

rows.pali: Aṭṭha kāmāvacarasahetukacittāni vuttanayeneva chasu dvāresu tadārammaṇakiccaṃ, attanā dinnapaṭisandhito uddhaṃ asati bhavaṅgupacchedake cittuppāde bhavaṅgakiccaṃ, ante cutikiccañcāti tīṇi kiccāni sādhayamānāni niyatavatthukāni aniyatadvārārammaṇaṭṭhānakiccāni hutvā pavattanti.
rows.english: 131. The eight sense-sphere consciousnesses with root-cause ((42)–(49)) occur accomplishing three functions, namely, the function of registration (m) in the six doors in the way already stated, the function of life-continuum (b) after rebirth- linking given by themselves, as long as there is no thought-arising to interrupt the life-continuum, and lastly the function of death (n) at the end. And they are invariable as to [possession of heart-] basis, and variable as to door, position, and function.

rows.pali: Pañca rūpāvacarāni cattāri ca āruppāni attanā dinnapaṭisandhito uddhaṃ asati bhavaṅgupacchedake cittuppāde bhavaṅgakiccaṃ, ante cutikiccañcāti kiccadvayaṃ sādhayamānāni pavattanti.
rows.english: 132. The five fine-material consciousnesses ((57)–(61)) and the four immaterial consciousnesses ((62)–(65)) occur accomplishing two functions, namely, the function of life-continuum (b) that continues after rebirth-linking given by themselves, as long as there is no thought-arising to interrupt the life-continuum, and the function of death (n) at the end.

rows.pali: Tesu rūpāvacarāni niyatavatthārammaṇāni aniyataṭṭhānakiccāni, itarāni niyatavatthukāni niyatārammaṇāni aniyataṭṭhānakiccāni hutvā pavattantīti evaṃ tāva bāttiṃsavidhampi viññāṇaṃ pavattiyaṃ saṅkhārapaccayā pavattati.
rows.english: As regards these, those of the fine- material sphere are invariable as to [possession of heart-]basis and as to their object, and they are variable as to position and function, while the others occur invariably without [heart-] basis, and they are invariable as to object, and variable as to position and function. This, in the first place, is how the thirty-twofold resultant consciousness occurs in the course of an individual existence with formations as condition.

rows.pali: Tatrāssa te te saṅkhārā kammapaccayena ca upanissayapaccayena ca paccayā honti.
rows.english: And there [in the course of an existence] these several formations are conditions, as kamma condition and decisive-support condition, for this [thirty-twofold resultant consciousness].

rows.pali: 622.Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "sesesu ekūnavīsatiyā na kiñci attano anurūpāya paṭisandhiyā na pavattatī"ti, taṃ atisaṃkhittattā dubbijānaṃ.
rows.english: 133. But what was said above, namely, “as to the remaining nineteen, there is none that does not occur as a rebirth-linking appropriate to it” (§130), is hard to understand since it is too brief.

rows.pali: Tenassa vitthāranayadassanatthaṃ vuccati – kati paṭisandhiyo, kati paṭisandhicittāni, kena kattha paṭisandhi hoti, kiṃ paṭisandhiyā ārammaṇanti?
rows.english: Hence, in order to show the details it may be asked: (i) How many kinds of rebirth-linking are there? (ii) How many kinds of rebirth- linking consciousness? (iii) Where and by what means does rebirth-linking come about? (iv) What does rebirth-linking [consciousness] have as its object?

rows.pali: Asaññapaṭisandhiyā saddhiṃ vīsati paṭisandhiyo.
rows.english: 134. (i) Including the rebirth-linking of non-percipient beings there are twenty kinds of rebirth-linking.

rows.pali: Vuttappakārāneva ekūnavīsati paṭisandhicittāni.
rows.english: (ii) There are nineteen kinds of rebirth-linking consciousnesses, as already described.

rows.pali: Tattha akusalavipākāya ahetukamanoviññāṇadhātuyā apāyesu paṭisandhi hoti.
rows.english: (iii) Herein, rebirth-linking by means of the unprofitable-resultant root- causeless mind-consciousness element (56) comes about in the states of loss.

rows.pali: Kusalavipākāya manussaloke jaccandhajātibadhirajātiummattakajātieḷamūganapuṃsakādīnaṃ.
rows.english: Rebirth-linking by means of the profitable-resultant (41) comes about in the human world among those blind from birth, born deaf, born mad, born drivelling (see M I 20; M-a I 118), the sexless, and so on.

rows.pali: Aṭṭhahi sahetukakāmāvacaravipākehi kāmāvacaradevesu ceva manussesu ca puññavantānaṃ paṭisandhi hoti.
rows.english: Rebirth-linking by means of the eight principal resultant consciousnesses with root-cause ((42)–(49)) comes about among deities of the sense sphere and the meritorious among men.

rows.pali: Pañcahi rūpāvacaravipākehi rūpībrahmaloke.
rows.english: That by means of the five fine-material resultant kinds comes about in the fine-material Brahmā-world.

rows.pali: Catūhi arūpāvacaravipākehi arūpaloketi.
rows.english: That by means of the four immaterial-sphere resultant kinds comes about in the immaterial world.

rows.pali: Yena ca yattha paṭisandhi hoti, sā eva tassa anurūpā paṭisandhi nāma.
rows.english: So rebirth-linking [consciousness] conforms to the means by which, and the place in which, it comes about.

rows.pali: Saṅkhepato pana paṭisandhiyā tīṇi ārammaṇāni honti atītaṃ paccuppannaṃ navattabbañca.
rows.english: (iv) Briefly, rebirth-linking [consciousness] has three kinds of objects, namely, past, present, and not-so-classifiable (see Ch. III, n. 32).

rows.pali: Asaññā paṭisandhi anārammaṇāti.
rows.english: Non-percipient rebirth- linking [consciousness] has no object.

rows.pali: Tattha viññāṇañcāyatananevasaññānāsaññāyatanapaṭisandhīnaṃ atītameva ārammaṇaṃ.
rows.english: 135. Herein, in the base consisting of boundless consciousness and the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception, the object of rebirth-linking is past.

rows.pali: Dasannaṃ kāmāvacarānaṃ atītaṃ vā paccuppannaṃ vā.
rows.english: That of the ten kinds of sense-sphere rebirth-linking is past or present.

rows.pali: Sesānaṃ navattabbameva.
rows.english: That of the rest is not-so-classifiable.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tīsu ārammaṇesu pavattamānā pana paṭisandhi yasmā atītārammaṇassa vā navattabbārammaṇassa vā cuticittassa anantarameva pavattati.
rows.english: But while the rebirth-linking consciousness occurs thus with three kinds of objects, the death consciousness, next to which it occurs, has only a past or a not-so-classifiable object,

rows.pali: Paccuppannārammaṇaṃ pana cuticittaṃ nāma natthi.
rows.english: there being no death consciousness with a present object.

rows.pali: Tasmā dvīsu ārammaṇesu aññatarārammaṇāya cutiyā anantarā tīsu ārammaṇesu aññatarārammaṇāya paṭisandhiyā sugatiduggativasena pavattanākāro veditabbo.
rows.english: Consequently, it should be understood how it occurs in the happy destinies and the unhappy destinies as follows, that is to say, how rebirth-linking consciousness with one of three kinds of objects occurs next to death consciousness with one of two kinds of objects.

rows.pali: 623.Seyyathidaṃ – kāmāvacarasugatiyaṃ tāva ṭhitassa pāpakammino puggalassa "tānissa tasmiṃ samaye olambantī"tiādivacanato (ma. ni. 3.248) maraṇamañce nipannassa yathūpacitaṃ pāpakammaṃ vā kammanimittaṃ vā manodvāre āpāthamāgacchati.
rows.english: 136.[From happy to unhappy destiny.] For example, firstly in the case of a person in the happy destinies of the sense-sphere who is an evildoer, when he is lying on his deathbed, his evil kamma according as it has been stored up, or its sign, comes into focus in the mind door. For it is said, “Then [the evil deeds that he did in the past] … cover him [and overspread him and envelop him]” (M III 164), and so on.

rows.pali: Taṃ ārabbha uppannāya tadārammaṇapariyosānāya javanavīthiyā anantaraṃ bhavaṅgavisayaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā cuticittaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: Then next to the cognitive series of impulsions ending in registration22 that arose contingent upon that [kamma or its sign], death consciousness arises making the life-continuum’s objective field its object.
