rows.pali: Upakkhalitassa patanaṃ viya saṅkhārapaccayā viññāṇaṃ.
rows.english: consciousness with formations as condition is like the stumbler’s falling;

rows.pali: Patitassa gaṇḍapātubhāvo viya viññāṇapaccayā nāmarūpaṃ.
rows.english: mentality-materiality with consciousness as condition is like the appearance of a tumour on the fallen man;

rows.pali: Gaṇḍabhedapīḷakā viya nāmarūpapaccayā saḷāyatanaṃ.
rows.english: the sixfold base with mentality-materiality as condition is like a gathering that makes the tumour burst;

rows.pali: Gaṇḍapīḷakāghaṭṭanaṃ viya saḷāyatanapaccayā phasso.
rows.english: contact with the sixfold base as condition is like hitting the gathering in the tumour;

rows.pali: Ghaṭṭanadukkhaṃ viya phassapaccayā vedanā, dukkhassa paṭikārābhilāso viya vedanāpaccayā taṇhā.
rows.english: feeling with contact as condition is like the pain due to the blow; craving with feeling as condition is like longing for a remedy;

rows.pali: Paṭikārābhilāsena asappāyaggahaṇaṃ viya taṇhāpaccayā upādānaṃ.
rows.english: clinging with craving as condition is like seizing what is unsuitable through longing for a remedy; [583]

rows.pali: Upādiṇṇaasappāyālepanaṃ viya upādānapaccayā bhavo.
rows.english: becoming with clinging as condition is like applying the unsuitable remedy seized;

rows.pali: Asappāyālepanena gaṇḍavikārapātubhāvo viya bhavapaccayā jāti.
rows.english: birth with becoming as condition is like the appearance of a change [for the worse] in the tumour owing to the application of the unsuitable remedy;

rows.pali: Gaṇḍavikārato gaṇḍabhedo viya jātipaccayā jarāmaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: and ageing-and-death with birth as condition is like the bursting of the tumour after the change.

rows.pali: Yasmā vā panettha avijjā appaṭipattimicchāpaṭipattibhāvena satte abhibhavati paṭalaṃ viya akkhīni.
rows.english: Or again, ignorance here as “no theory” and “wrong theory” (see §52) befogs beings as a cataract does the eyes;

rows.pali: Tadabhibhūto ca bālo punabbhavikehi saṅkhārehi attānaṃ veṭheti kosakārakimi viya kosappadesehi.
rows.english: the fool befogged by it involves himself in formations that produce further becoming, as a cocoon-spinning caterpillar does with the strands of the cocoon;

rows.pali: Saṅkhārapariggahitaṃ viññāṇaṃ gatīsu patiṭṭhaṃ labhati pariṇāyakapariggahito viya rājakumāro rajje.
rows.english: consciousness guided by formations establishes itself in the destinies, as a prince guided by a minister establishes himself on a throne;

rows.pali: Upapattinimittaparikappanato viññāṇaṃ paṭisandhiyaṃ anekappakāraṃ nāmarūpaṃ abhinibbatteti māyākāro viya māyaṃ.
rows.english: [death] consciousness conjecturing about the sign of rebirth generates mentality-materiality in its various aspects in rebirth-linking, as a magician does an illusion;

rows.pali: Nāmarūpe patiṭṭhitaṃ saḷāyatanaṃ vuddhiṃ virūḷhiṃ vepullaṃ pāpuṇāti subhūmiyaṃ patiṭṭhito vanappagumbo viya.
rows.english: the sixfold base planted in mentality-materiality reaches growth, increase and fulfilment, as a forest thicket does planted in good soil;

rows.pali: Āyatanaghaṭṭanato phasso jāyati araṇisahitābhimanthanato aggi viya.
rows.english: contact is born from the impingement of the bases, as fire is born from the rubbing together of fire sticks;

rows.pali: Phassena phuṭṭhassa vedanā pātubhavati agginā phuṭṭhassa dāho viya.
rows.english: feeling is manifested in one touched by contact, as burning is in one touched by fire;

rows.pali: Vedayamānassa taṇhā pavaḍḍhati loṇūdakaṃ pivato pipāsā viya.
rows.english: craving increases in one who feels, as thirst does in one who drinks salt water;

rows.pali: Tasito bhavesu abhilāsaṃ karoti pipāsito viya pānīye.
rows.english: one who is parched [with craving] conceives longing for the kinds of becoming, as a thirsty man does for drinks;

rows.pali: Tadassupādānaṃ, upādānena bhavaṃ upādiyati āmisalobhena maccho baḷisaṃ viya.
rows.english: that is his clinging; by clinging he clings to becoming as a fish does to the hook through greed for the bait;

rows.pali: Bhave sati jāti hoti bīje sati aṅkuro viya.
rows.english: when there is becoming there is birth, as when there is a seed there is a shoot;

rows.pali: Jātassa avassaṃ jarāmaraṇaṃ uppannassa rukkhassa patanaṃ viya.
rows.english: and death is certain for one who is born, as falling down is for a tree that has grown up.

rows.pali: Tasmā evaṃ upamāhipetaṃ bhavacakkaṃ viññātabbaṃ yathārahaṃ.
rows.english: So this Wheel of Becoming should be known thus “as to similes” too in whichever way is appropriate.

rows.pali: 659.Yasmā ca bhagavatā atthatopi dhammatopi desanatopi paṭivedhatopi gambhīrabhāvaṃ sandhāya "gambhīro cāyaṃ, ānanda, paṭiccasamuppādo gambhīrāvabhāso cā"ti (dī. ni. 2.95; saṃ. ni. 2.60) vuttaṃ, tasmā gambhīrabhedatopetaṃ bhavacakkaṃ viññātabbaṃ yathārahaṃ.
rows.english: 304. 5. [Kinds of profundity:] Now, the Blessed One’s words, “This dependent origination is profound, Ānanda, and profound it appears” (D II 55), refer to profundity (a) of meaning, (b) of law, (c) of teaching, and (d) of penetration. So this Wheel of Becoming should be known “as to the kinds of profundity” in whichever way is appropriate.

rows.pali: Tattha yasmā na jātito jarāmaraṇaṃ na hoti, na ca jātiṃ vinā aññato hoti, itthañca jātito samudāgacchatīti evaṃ jātipaccayasamudāgataṭṭhassa duravabodhanīyato jarāmaraṇassa jātipaccayasambhūtasamudāgataṭṭho gambhīro.
rows.english: 305. (a) Herein, the meaning of ageing-and-death produced and originated with birth as condition is profound owing to difficulty in understanding its origin with birth as condition thus: Neither does ageing-and death not come about from birth, nor, failing birth, does it come about from something else; it arises [only] from birth with precisely that nature [of ageing-and-death].

rows.pali: Tathā jātiyā bhavapaccaya - pe - saṅkhārānaṃ avijjāpaccayasambhūtasamudāgataṭṭho gambhīro.
rows.english: And the meaning of birth with becoming as condition … and the meaning of formations produced and originated with ignorance as condition are treatable in like manner.

rows.pali: Tasmā idaṃ bhavacakkaṃ atthagambhīranti ayaṃ tāvettha atthagambhīratā.
rows.english: That is why this Wheel of Becoming is profound in meaning. This, firstly, is the profundity of meaning here.

rows.pali: Hetuphalañhi atthoti vuccati.
rows.english: For it is the fruit of a cause that is called “meaning,”

rows.pali: Yathāha – "hetuphale ñāṇaṃ atthapaṭisambhidā"ti (vibha. 720).
rows.english: according as it is said, “Knowledge about the fruit of a cause is the discrimination of meaning” (Vibh 293).

rows.pali: Yasmā pana yenākārena yadavatthā ca avijjā tesaṃ tesaṃ saṅkhārānaṃ paccayo hoti, tassa duravabodhanīyato avijjāya saṅkhārānaṃ paccayaṭṭho gambhīro.
rows.english: 306. (b) The meaning of ignorance as condition for formations is profound since it is difficult to understand in what mode and on what occasion46 ignorance is a condition for the several formations …

rows.pali: Tathā saṅkhārānaṃ - pe - jātiyā jarāmaraṇassa paccayaṭṭho gambhīro, tasmā idaṃ bhavacakkaṃ dhammagambhīranti ayamettha dhammagambhīratā.
rows.english: The meaning of birth as a condition for ageing-and-death is similarly profound. That is why this Wheel of Becoming is profound in law. This is the profundity of law here.

rows.pali: Hetuno hi dhammoti nāmaṃ.
rows.english: For “law” is a name for cause,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "hetumhi ñāṇaṃ dhammapaṭisambhidā"ti (vibha. 720).
rows.english: according as it is said, “Knowledge about cause is discrimination of law” (Vibh 293).

rows.pali: Yasmā cassa tena tena kāraṇena tathā tathā pavattetabbattā desanāpi gambhīrā, na tattha sabbaññutaññāṇato aññaṃ ñāṇaṃ patiṭṭhaṃ labhati.
rows.english: 307. (c) Then the teaching of this [dependent origination] is profound since it needs to be given in various ways for various reasons, and none but omniscient knowledge gets fully established in it;

rows.pali: Tathāhetaṃ katthaci sutte anulomato, katthaci paṭilomato, katthaci anulomapaṭilomato, katthaci vemajjhato paṭṭhāya anulomato vā paṭilomato vā, katthaci tisandhicatusaṅkhepaṃ, katthaci dvisandhitisaṅkhepaṃ, katthaci ekasandhidvisaṅkhepaṃ desitaṃ, tasmā idaṃ bhavacakkaṃ desanāgambhīranti ayaṃ desanāgambhīratā.
rows.english: for in some places in the suttas it is taught in forward order, in some in backward order, in some in forward and backward order, in some in forward or in backward order starting from the middle, in some in four sections and three links, in some in three sections and two links, and in some in two sections and one link. That is why this Wheel of Becoming is profound in teaching. This is the profundity of teaching.

rows.pali: Yasmā cettha yo so avijjādīnaṃ sabhāvo, yena paṭividdhena avijjādayo sammā salakkhaṇato paṭividdhā honti, so duppariyogāhattā gambhīro, tasmā idaṃ bhavacakkaṃ paṭivedhagambhīraṃ.
rows.english: 308. (d) Then the individual essences of ignorance, etc., owing to the penetration of which ignorance, etc., are rightly penetrated as to their specific characteristic, are profound since they are difficult to fathom. That is why this Wheel of Becoming is profound in penetration.

rows.pali: Tathā hettha avijjāya aññāṇādassanasaccāsampaṭivedhaṭṭho gambhīro, saṅkhārānaṃ abhisaṅkharaṇāyūhanasarāgavirāgaṭṭho, viññāṇassa suññataabyāpāraasaṅkantipaṭisandhipātubhāvaṭṭho, nāmarūpassa ekuppādavinibbhogāvinibbhoganamanaruppanaṭṭho, saḷāyatanassa adhipatilokadvārakhettavisayibhāvaṭṭho, phassassa phusanasaṅghaṭṭanasaṅgatisannipātaṭṭho, vedanāya ārammaṇarasānubhavanasukhadukkhamajjhattabhāvanijjīvavedayitaṭṭho.
rows.english: For here the meaning of ignorance as unknowing and unseeing and non-penetration of the truth is profound; so is the meaning of formations as forming and accumulating with and without greed; so is the meaning of consciousness as void, uninterested, and manifestation of rebirth-linking without transmigration; so is the meaning of mentality-materiality as simultaneous arising, as resolved into components or not, and as bending [on to an object] (namana) and being molested (ruppana); so is the meaning of the sixfold base as predominance, world, door, field, and possession of objective field; so is the meaning of contact as touching, impingement, coincidence, and concurrence; so is the meaning of feeling as the experiencing of the stimulus of an object, as pleasure or pain or neutrality, as soulless, and as what is felt;

rows.pali: Taṇhāya abhinanditajjhosānasaritālatānadītaṇhāsamuddaduppūraṭṭho, upādānassa ādānaggahaṇābhinivesaparāmāsaduratikkamaṭṭho, bhavassa āyūhanābhisaṅkharaṇayonigatiṭhitinivāsesukhipanaṭṭho, jātiyā jāti sañjāti okkanti nibbatti pātubhāvaṭṭho, jarāmaraṇassa khayavayabhedavipariṇāmaṭṭho gambhīroti ayamettha paṭivedhagambhīratā.
rows.english: so is the meaning of craving as a delighting in, as a committal to, as a current, as a bindweed, as a river, as the ocean of craving, and as impossible to fill; so is the meaning of clinging as grasping, seizing, misinterpreting, adhering, and hard to get by; so is the meaning of becoming as accumulating, forming, and flinging into the various kinds of generation, destiny, station, and abode; so is the meaning of birth as birth, coming to birth, descent [into the womb], rebirth, and manifestation; and so is the meaning of ageing- and-birth as destruction, fall, break-up and change. This is profundity of penetration.

rows.pali: 660.Yasmā panettha ekattanayo, nānattanayo, abyāpāranayo, evaṃdhammatānayoti cattāro atthanayā honti, tasmā nayabhedatopetaṃ bhavacakkaṃ viññātabbaṃ yathārahaṃ.
rows.english: 309.6. [As to methods:] Then [585] there are four methods of treating the meaning here. They are (a) the method of identity, (b) the method of diversity, (c) the method of uninterest,47 and (d) the method of ineluctable regularity. So this Wheel of Becoming should also be known accordingly “as to the kinds of method. ”48

rows.pali: Tattha avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā, saṅkhārapaccayā viññāṇanti evaṃ bījassa aṅkurādibhāvena rukkhabhāvappatti viya santānānupacchedo ekattanayo nāma.
rows.english: 310. (a) Herein, the non-interruption of the continuity in this way, “With ignorance as condition there are formations; with formations as condition, consciousness,” just like a seed’s reaching the state of a tree through the state of the shoot, etc., is called the “method of identity.”

rows.pali: Yaṃ sammā passanto hetuphalasambandhena santānassa anupacchedāvabodhato ucchedadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: One who sees this rightly abandons the annihilation view by understanding the unbrokenness of the continuity that occurs through the linking of cause and fruit.

rows.pali: Micchā passanto hetuphalasambandhena pavattamānassa santānānupacchedassa ekattagahaṇato sassatadiṭṭhiṃ upādiyati.
rows.english: And one who sees it wrongly clings to the eternity view by apprehending identity in the non- interruption of the continuity that occurs through the linking of cause and fruit.

rows.pali: Avijjādīnaṃ pana yathāsakaṃlakkhaṇavavatthānaṃ nānattanayo nāma.
rows.english: 311. (b) The defining of the individual characteristic of ignorance, etc., is called the “method of diversity.”

rows.pali: Yaṃ sammā passanto navanavānaṃ uppādadassanato sassatadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: One who sees this rightly abandons the eternity view by seeing the arising of each new state.

rows.pali: Micchā passanto ekasantānapatitassa bhinnasantānasseva nānattaggahaṇato ucchedadiṭṭhiṃ upādiyati.
rows.english: And one who sees it wrongly clings to the annihilation view by apprehending individual diversity in the events in a single continuity as though it were a broken continuity.

rows.pali: Avijjāya saṅkhārā mayā uppādetabbā, saṅkhārānaṃ vā viññāṇaṃ amhehīti evamādibyāpārābhāvo abyāpāranayo nāma.
rows.english: 312. (c) The absence of interestedness on the part of ignorance, such as “Formations must be made to occur by me,” or on the part of formations, such as “Consciousness must be made to occur by us,” and so on, is called the “method of uninterestedness.”

rows.pali: Yaṃ sammā passanto kārakassa abhāvāvabodhato attadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: One who sees this rightly abandons the self view by understanding the absence of a maker.

rows.pali: Micchā passanto yo asatipi byāpāre avijjādīnaṃ sabhāvaniyamasiddho hetubhāvo, tassa aggahaṇato akiriyadiṭṭhiṃ upādiyati.
rows.english: One who sees it wrongly clings to the moral-inefficacy-of-action view, because he does not perceive that the causative function of ignorance, etc., is established as a law by their respective individual essences.

rows.pali: Avijjādīhi pana kāraṇehi saṅkhārādīnaṃyeva sambhavo khīrādīhi dadhiādīnaṃ viya, na aññesanti ayaṃ evaṃdhammatānayo nāma.
rows.english: 313. (d) The production of only formations, etc., respectively and no others with ignorance, etc., as the respective reasons, like that of curd, etc., with milk, etc., as the respective reasons, is called the “method of ineluctable regularity.”

rows.pali: Yaṃ sammā passanto paccayānurūpato phalāvabodhā ahetukadiṭṭhiṃ akiriyadiṭṭhiñca pajahati.
rows.english: One who sees this rightly abandons the no-cause view and the moral-inefficacy- of-action view by understanding how the fruit accords with its condition.

rows.pali: Micchā passanto paccayānurūpaṃ phalappavattiṃ aggahetvā yato kutoci yassa kassaci asambhavaggahaṇato ahetukadiṭṭhiñceva niyatavādañca upādiyatīti evamidaṃ bhavacakkaṃ,
rows.english: One who sees it wrongly by apprehending it as non-production of anything from anything, instead of apprehending the occurrence of the fruit in accordance with its conditions, clings to the no-cause view and to the doctrine of fatalism. So this Wheel of Becoming:

rows.pali: 661.Idañhi atigambhīrato agādhaṃ.
rows.english: 314. There is no one, even in a dream, who has got out of the fearful round of rebirths, which is ever destroying like a thunderbolt, unless he has severed with the knife of knowledge well whetted on the stone of sublime concentration, this Wheel of Becoming, which offers no footing owing to its great profundity and is hard to get by owing to the maze of many methods.

rows.pali: Nānānayagahanato duratiyānaṃ.
Ñāṇāsinā samādhipavarasilāyaṃ sunisitena,
Vuttampi hetaṃ bhagavatā – "gambhīro cāyaṃ, ānanda, paṭiccasamuppādo gambhīrāvabhāso ca.
rows.english: And this has been said by the Blessed One: “This dependent origination is profound, Ānanda, and profound it appears.

rows.pali: Etassa cānanda, dhammassa ananubodhā appaṭivedhā evamayaṃ pajā tantākulakajātā kulāgaṇṭhikajātā muñjapabbajabhūtā apāyaṃ duggatiṃ vinipātaṃ saṃsāraṃ nātivattatī"ti (mahāva. 95; saṃ. ni. 2.60).
rows.english: And, Ānanda, it is through not knowing, through not penetrating it, that this generation has become a tangled skein, a knotted ball of thread, root-matted as a reed bed, and finds no way out of the round of rebirths, with its states of loss, unhappy destinies, … perdition” (D II 55).

rows.pali: Tasmā attano vā paresaṃ vā hitāya ca sukhāya ca paṭipanno avasesakiccāni pahāya,
rows.english: Therefore, practicing for his own and others’ benefit and welfare, and abandoning other duties:

rows.pali: 570.Idāni "khandhāyatanadhātuindriyasaccapaṭiccasamuppādādibhedā dhammā bhūmī"ti evaṃ vuttesu imissā paññāya bhūmibhūtesu dhammesu yasmā paṭiccasamuppādoceva, ādisaddena saṅgahitā paṭiccasamuppannā dhammā ca avasesā honti, tasmā tesaṃ vaṇṇanākkamo anuppatto.
rows.english: 1.The turn has now come for the exposition of the dependent origination itself, and the dependently-originated states comprised by the word “etc.,” since these still remain out of the states called the “soil” (bhūmi), of which it was said above, “The states classed as aggregates, bases, elements, faculties, truths, and dependent origination, etc., are the ‘soil’” (XIV.32).

rows.pali: Tattha avijjādayo tāva dhammā paṭiccasamuppādoti veditabbā.
rows.english: 2. Herein, firstly, it is the states beginning with ignorance that should be understood as dependent origination.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One:

rows.pali: Jarāmaraṇādayo pana paṭiccasamuppannā dhammāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 3. Secondly, it is the states beginning with ageing-and-death that should be understood as dependently-originated states.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One:

rows.pali: 571.Ayaṃ panettha saṅkhepo.
rows.english: 4. Here is a brief explanation.

rows.pali: Paṭiccasamuppādoti paccayadhammā veditabbā.
rows.english: The states that are conditions should be understood as the dependent origination.

rows.pali: Paṭiccasamuppannā dhammāti tehi tehi paccayehi nibbattadhammā.
rows.english: The states generated by such and such conditions are dependently-originated states.

rows.pali: Kathamidaṃ jānitabbanti ce?
rows.english: 5.How is that to be known?

rows.pali: Bhagavato vacanena.
rows.english: By the Blessed One’s word.

rows.pali: Bhagavatā hi paṭiccasamuppādapaṭiccasamuppannadhammadesanāsutte –
rows.english: For it is precisely those states which are conditions, that with the synonyms beginning with “reality” have been called “dependent origination” by the Blessed One when teaching the dependent origination in the sutta on the Teaching of the Dependent Origination and Dependently-originated States thus:

rows.pali: 572.Evaṃ paṭiccasamuppādaṃ desentena tathatādīhi vevacanehi paccayadhammāva paṭiccasamuppādoti vuttā.
Tasmā jarāmaraṇādīnaṃ dhammānaṃ paccayalakkhaṇo paṭiccasamuppādo, dukkhānubandhanaraso, kummaggapaccupaṭṭhānoti veditabbo.
rows.english: Consequently, it should be understood that dependent origination has the characteristic of being the conditions for the states beginning with ageing-and- death. Its function is to continue [the process of] suffering. It is manifested as the wrong path.

rows.pali: So panāyaṃ tehi tehi paccayehi anūnādhikeheva tassa tassa dhammassa sambhavato tathatāti, sāmaggiṃ upagatesu paccayesu muhuttampi tato nibbattadhammānaṃ asambhavābhāvato avitathatāti, aññadhammapaccayehi aññadhammānuppattito anaññathatāti, yathāvuttānaṃ etesaṃ jarāmaraṇādīnaṃ paccayato vā paccayasamūhato vā idappaccayatāti vutto.
rows.english: 6. Because particular states are produced by particular conditions, neither less nor more, it is called reality (suchness). Because once the conditions have met in combination there is no non-producing, even for an instant, of the states they generate, it is called not unreality (not unsuchness). Because there is no arising of one state with another state’s conditions, it is called not otherness. Because there is a condition, or because there is a total of conditions, for these states beginning with ageing-and-death as already stated, it is called specific conditionality.

rows.pali: 573.Tatrāyaṃ vacanattho, imesaṃ paccayā idappaccayā.
rows.english: 7.Here is the word meaning: idappaccayā (lit. that-conditions) = imesaṃ paccayā (conditions for those);

rows.pali: Idappaccayā eva idappaccayatā.
rows.english: idappaccayā (that-conditions) = idappaccayatā (that-conditionality, conditionality for those, specific conditionality).

rows.pali: Idappaccayānaṃ vā samūho idappaccayatā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, idappaccayatā (that-conditionality) = idappaccayānaṃ samūho (the total of that- conditions, total specific conditionality).

rows.pali: Lakkhaṇaṃ panettha saddasatthato pariyesitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 8. The characteristic must be sought from grammar.

rows.pali: 574.Keci pana paṭicca sammā ca titthiyaparikappitapakatipurisādikāraṇanirapekkho uppādo paṭiccasamuppādoti evaṃ uppādamattaṃ paṭiccasamuppādoti vadanti, taṃ na yujjati.
rows.english: Some, in fact, [say that the expression paṭicca samuppāda (dependent origination) is characterized thus:] “having depended (paṭicca), a right (sammā) arising (uppāda), [depending on causes rightly by] disregarding such causes conjectured by sectarians as the Primordial Essence (Prakriti), World Soul (Purusa), and so on. ” So what they call dependent origination (paṭicca samuppāda) is a simple arising (uppāda) [for they equate the prefix saṃ only with sammā (rightly) and ignore saṃ (with, con-)]. That is untenable.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
Suttābhāvato, suttavirodhato, gambhīranayāsambhavato, saddabhedato ca.
rows.english: (1) There is no such sutta; (2) it contradicts suttas; (3) it admits of no profound treatment; and (4) it is ungrammatical.

rows.pali: "Uppādamattaṃ paṭiccasamuppādo"ti hi suttaṃ natthi.
rows.english: 9.(1) No sutta describes the dependent origination as simple arising.

rows.pali: Taṃ "paṭiccasamuppādo"ti ca vadantassa padesavihārasuttavirodho āpajjati.
rows.english: (2) Anyone who asserts that dependent origination is of that kind involves himself in conflict with the Padesavihāra Sutta.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Bhagavato hi "atha kho bhagavā rattiyā paṭhamaṃ yāmaṃ paṭiccasamuppādaṃ anulomapaṭilomaṃ manasākāsī"ti (mahāva. 1) ādivacanato paṭiccasamuppādamanasikāro paṭhamābhisambuddhavihāro, padesavihāro ca tassekadesavihāro.
rows.english: The Newly Enlightened One’s abiding (vihāra) is the bringing of the dependent origination to mind, because, of these words of the Blessed One’s: “Then in the first watch of the night the Blessed One brought to mind the dependent origination in direct and reverse order” [as origination and cessation] (Vin I 1; Ud 2). Now, “padesavihāra” is the abiding (vihāra) in one part (desa) of that,

rows.pali: Yathāha "yena svāhaṃ, bhikkhave, vihārena paṭhamābhisambuddho viharāmi, tassa padesena vihāsi"nti (saṃ. ni. 5.11).
rows.english: according as it is said, “Bhikkhus, I abode in a part of the abiding in which I abode when I was newly enlightened” (S V 12; Paṭis I 107).

rows.pali: Tatra ca paccayākāradassanena vihāsi, na uppādamattadassanenāti.
rows.english: And there he abode in the vision of structure of conditions, not in the vision of simple arising,

rows.pali: Yathāha "so evaṃ pajānāmi micchādiṭṭhipaccayāpi vedayitaṃ sammādiṭṭhipaccayāpi vedayitaṃ micchāsaṅkappapaccayāpi vedayita"nti (saṃ. ni. 5.11) sabbaṃ vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: according as it is said, “So I understood feeling with wrong view as its condition, and feeling with right view as its condition, and feeling with wrong thinking as its condition …” (S V 12), all of which should be quoted in full.

rows.pali: Evaṃ uppādamattaṃ "paṭiccasamuppādo"ti vadantassa padesavihārasuttavirodho āpajjati.
rows.english: So anyone who asserts that dependent origination is simple arising involves himself in conflict with the Padesavihāra Sutta.

rows.pali: Tathā kaccānasuttavirodho.
rows.english: 10. There is likewise contradiction of the Kaccāna Sutta.

rows.pali: Kaccānasuttepi hi "lokasamudayaṃ kho, kaccāna, yathābhūtaṃ sammappaññāya passato yā loke natthitā, sā na hotī"ti (saṃ. ni. 2.15) anulomapaṭiccasamuppādo lokapaccayato "lokasamudayo"ti ucchedadiṭṭhisamugghātatthaṃ pakāsito, na uppādamattaṃ.
rows.english: For in the Kaccāna Sutta it is said, “When a man sees correctly with right understanding the origination of the world, Kaccāna, he does not say of the world that it is not” (S II 17). And there it is the dependent origination in forward order, not simple arising, that, as the origination of the world from its conditions, is set forth in order to eliminate the annihilation view.

rows.pali: Na hi uppādamattadassanena ucchedadiṭṭhiyā samugghāto hoti.
rows.english: For the annihilation view is not eliminated by seeing simple arising;

rows.pali: Paccayānuparamadassanena pana hoti.
rows.english: but it is eliminated by seeing the chain of conditions

rows.pali: Paccayānuparame phalānuparamatoti.
rows.english: as a chain of fruits following on a chain of conditions.

rows.pali: Evaṃ uppādamattaṃ "paṭiccasamuppādo"ti vadantassa kaccānasuttavirodhopi āpajjati.
rows.english: So anyone who asserts that the dependent origination is simple arising involves himself in contradiction of the Kaccāna Sutta.

rows.pali: Gambhīranayāsambhavatoti vuttaṃ kho panetaṃ bhagavatā "gambhīro cāyaṃ, ānanda, paṭiccasamuppādo gambhīrāvabhāso cā"ti (dī. ni. 2.95; saṃ. ni. 2.60).
rows.english: 11.(3) It admits of no profound treatment: this has been said by the Blessed One, “This dependent origination is profound, Ānanda, and profound it appears” (D II 55; S II 92).

rows.pali: Gambhīrattañca nāma catubbidhaṃ, taṃ parato vaṇṇayissāma.
rows.english: And the profundity is fourfold as we shall explain below (XVII.304f.);

rows.pali: Taṃ uppādamatte natthi.
rows.english: but there is none of that in simple arising.

rows.pali: Catubbidhanayapaṭimaṇḍitañcetaṃ paṭiccasamuppādaṃ vaṇṇayanti, tampi nayacatukkaṃ uppādamatte natthīti gambhīranayāsambhavatopi na uppādamattaṃ paṭiccasamuppādo.
rows.english: And this dependent origination is explained [by the teachers] as adorned with the fourfold method (XVII.309); but there is no [need of] any such tetrad of methods in simple arising. So dependent origination is not simple arising, since that admits of no profound treatment.

rows.pali: 575.Saddabhedatoti paṭiccasaddo ca panāyaṃ samāne kattari pubbakāle payujjamāno atthasiddhikaro hoti.
rows.english: 12.(4) It is ungrammatical: [520] this word paṭicca (lit. “having depended”; freely “due to,” “dependent”), [being a gerund of the verb paṭi + eti, to go back to], establishes a meaning [in a formula of establishment by verb] when it is construed as past with the same subject [as that of the principal verb],

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, "cakkhuñca paṭicca rūpe ca uppajjati cakkhuviññāṇa"nti (saṃ. ni. 2.43).
rows.english: as in the sentence “Having depended on (paṭicca = ‘due to’) the eye and visible objects, eye- consciousness arises (uppajjati)” (S II 72).

rows.pali: Idha pana bhāvasādhanena uppādasaddena saddhiṃ payujjamāno samānassa kattu abhāvato saddabhedaṃ gacchati, na ca kiñci atthaṃ sādhetīti saddabhedatopi na uppādamattaṃ paṭiccasamuppādoti.
rows.english: But if it is construed here with the word uppāda (arising), [which is a noun], in a formula of establishment by noun, there is a breach of grammar, because there is no shared subject [as there is in above-quoted sentence], and so it does not establish any meaning al all. So the dependent origination is not simple arising because that is ungrammatical.

rows.pali: Tattha siyā – "hoti-saddena saddhiṃ yojayissāma 'paṭiccasamuppādo hotī'ti", taṃ na yuttaṃ.
rows.english: 13. Here it might be [argued]: “We shall add the words ‘comes to be’ (hoti) thus: ‘Having depended, arising comes to be’ (paṭicca, samuppādo hoti). ” That will not do.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why not?

rows.pali: Yogābhāvato ceva, uppādassa ca uppādapattidosato.
rows.english: Because there is no instance in which it has been added, and because the fallacy of the arising of an arising follows.

rows.pali: "Paṭiccasamuppādaṃ vo, bhikkhave, desessāmi.
rows.english: For in such passages as “Paṭiccasamuppādaṃ vo bhikkhave desessāmi. (I shall teach you the dependent origination, bhikkhus.

rows.pali: Katamo ca, bhikkhave, paṭiccasamuppādo - pe - ayaṃ vuccati, bhikkhave, paṭiccasamuppādo"ti (saṃ. ni. 2.1).
rows.english: Katamo ca bhikkhave paṭiccasamuppādo (And what is the dependent origination?) … Ayaṃ vuccati bhikkhave paṭiccasamuppādo … This is called the dependent origination, bhikkhus)” (S II 1),

rows.pali: Imesu hi padesu ekenapi saddhiṃ hoti-saddo yogaṃ na gacchati, na ca uppādo hoti.
rows.english: the words “comes to be” (hoti) are not added in any single instance. And there is no [such expression as] “arising comes to be”:

rows.pali: Sace bhaveyya, uppādassāpi uppādo pāpuṇeyyāti.
rows.english: if there were, it would be tantamount to saying that arising itself had an arising too.

rows.pali: 576.Yepi maññanti "idappaccayānaṃ bhāvo idappaccayatā, bhāvo ca nāma yo ākāro avijjādīnaṃ saṅkhārādipātubhāve hetu, so.
rows.english: 14. And those are wrong who imagine that specific conditionality (idappaccayatā) is the specific conditions’ [abstract] essence—what is called “abstract essence” being a [particular] mode in ignorance, etc., that acts as cause in the manifestation of formations, etc.—

rows.pali: Tasmiñca saṅkhāravikāre paṭiccasamuppādasaññā"ti, tesaṃ taṃ na yujjati.
rows.english: and that the term “dependent origination” is used for an alteration in formations when there is that [particular mode in the way of occurrence of ignorance].

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why are they wrong?

rows.pali: Avijjādīnaṃ hetuvacanato.
rows.english: Because it is ignorance, etc., themselves that are called causes.

rows.pali: Bhagavatā hi "tasmātiha, ānanda, eseva hetu, etaṃ nidānaṃ, esa samudayo, esa paccayo jarāmaraṇassa yadidaṃ jāti - pe - saṅkhārānaṃ, yadidaṃ avijjā"ti (dī. ni. 2.98 ādayo) evaṃ avijjādayova hetūti vuttā, na tesaṃ vikāro.
rows.english: For in the following passage it is ignorance, etc., themselves, not their alteration, that are called the causes [of these states]: “Therefore, Ānanda, just this is the cause, this is the source, this is the origin, this is the condition, for ageing-and-death, that is to say, birth … for formations, that is to say, (ignorance)” (D II 57–63—the last clause is not in the Dīgha text).

rows.pali: Tasmā "paṭiccasamuppādoti paccayadhammā veditabbā"ti iti yaṃ taṃ vuttaṃ, taṃ sammā vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Therefore it is the actual states themselves as conditions that should be understood as “dependent origination. ” So what was said above (§4) can be understood as rightly said.

rows.pali: 577.Yā panettha "paṭiccasamuppādo"ti imāya byañjanacchāyāya uppādoyevāyaṃ vuttoti saññā uppajjati, sā imassa padassa evamatthaṃ gahetvā vūpasametabbā.
rows.english: 15. If any notion arises in the guise of a literal interpretation of the term “dependent origination” (paṭicca-samuppāda) to the effect that it is only arising that is stated, it should be got rid of by apprehending the meaning of this expression in the following way.

rows.pali: Bhagavatā hi,
rows.english: For: In double form this term relates to a totality of state Produced from a conditionality; Hence the conditions for that sum Through metaphor’s device have come To bear their fruits’ name figuratively In the Blessed One’s exposition.

rows.pali: Yo hi ayaṃ paccayatāya pavatto dhammasamūho, tattha paṭiccasamuppādoti idaṃ vacanaṃ dvidhā icchanti.
rows.english: 16. This term “dependent origination,” when applied to the total of states produced from the [total] conditionality, must be taken in two ways.

rows.pali: So hi yasmā patīyamāno hitāya sukhāya ca saṃvattati, tasmā paccetumarahanti naṃ paṇḍitāti paṭicco.
rows.english: For that [total] ought to be arrived at (paṭicco—adj.),2 since when it is arrived at (paṭiyamāno), it leads to [supramundane] welfare and bliss and so the wise [regard] it as worthy to be arrived at (paccetuṃ);

rows.pali: Uppajjamāno ca saha sammā ca uppajjati, na ekekato, nāpi ahetutoti samuppādo.
rows.english: and then, when it arises (uppajjamāno), it does so “together with” (saha) and “rightly” (sammā), not singly or causelessly, thus it is a co-arising (samuppādo).

rows.pali: Evaṃ paṭicco ca so samuppādo cāti paṭiccasamuppādo.
rows.english: Consequently: it is to be arrived at (paṭicco) and it is a co-arising (samuppādo), thus it is dependent origination (paṭicca-samuppāda).

rows.pali: Apica saha uppajjatīti samuppādo, paccayasāmaggiṃ pana paṭicca apaccakkhāyāti evampi paṭiccasamuppādo.
rows.english: Again: it arises as a togetherness (saha), thus it is a co- arising (samuppāda); but it does so having depended (paṭicca—ger.) in combination with conditions, not regardless of them. Consequently: it, having depended (paṭicca), is a co-arising (samuppāda), thus in this way also it is dependent origination (paṭicca-samuppāda).

rows.pali: Tassa cāyaṃ hetusamūho paccayoti tappaccayattā ayampi, yathā loke semhassa paccayo guḷo semho guḷoti vuccati, yathā ca sāsane sukhappaccayo buddhānaṃ uppādo "sukho buddhānaṃ uppādo"ti vuccati, tathā paṭiccasamuppādo icceva phalavohārena vuttoti veditabbo.
rows.english: And the total of causes is a condition for that [total of states produced from the conditionality], so, because it is a condition for that, this [total of causes] is called, “dependent origination,” using for it the term ordinarily used for its fruit just as in the world molasses, which is a condition for phlegm, is spoken of thus, “Molasses is phlegm,” or just as in the Dispensation the arising of Buddhas, which is a condition for bliss, is spoken of thus, “The arising of Buddhas is bliss” (Dhp 194).

rows.pali: 578.Atha vā,
rows.english: 17. Or alternatively:

rows.pali: Yo hi esa saṅkhārādīnaṃ pātubhāvāya avijjādiekekahetusīsena niddiṭṭho hetusamūho, so sādhāraṇaphalanipphādakaṭṭhena avekallaṭṭhena ca sāmaggiaṅgānaṃ aññamaññena paṭimukhaṃ ito gatoti katvā paṭiccoti vuccati.
rows.english: 18.This total of causes—indicated severally under the heading of each cause, beginning with ignorance—for the manifestation of formations, etc., is called “dependent” (paṭicco—adj.), taking it as “facing, gone to, its counterpart” (paṭimukham ito) owing to the mutual interdependence of the factors in the combination, in the sense both that they produce common fruit and that none can be dispensed with.

rows.pali: Svāyaṃ sahiteyeva aññamaññaṃ avinibbhogavuttidhamme uppādetīti samuppādotipi vutto.
rows.english: And it is called a “co-arising” (samuppādo) since it causes the states that occur in unresolved mutual interdependence to arise associatedly.

rows.pali: Evampi paṭicco ca so samuppādo cāti paṭiccasamuppādo.
rows.english: Consequently: it is dependent (paṭicco) and a co-arising (samuppādo), thus in this way also it is dependent origination (paṭicca-samuppāda).

rows.pali: 579.Aparo nayo –
rows.english: 19. Another method:

rows.pali: Avijjādisīsena niddiṭṭhapaccayesu hi ye paccayā yaṃ saṅkhārādikaṃ dhammaṃ uppādenti, na te aññamaññaṃ apaṭicca aññamaññavekalle sati uppādetuṃ samatthāti.
rows.english: 20.Among the conditions described under the headings of ignorance, etc., the respective conditions that make the [conditionally-arisen] states beginning with formations arise are incapable of making them arise when not mutually dependent and when deficient.

rows.pali: Tasmā paṭicca samaṃ saha ca na ekekadesaṃ, nāpi pubbāparabhāvena ayaṃ paccayatā dhamme uppādetīti atthānusāravohārakusalena muninā evamidha bhāsitā, paṭiccasamuppādotveva bhāsitāti attho.
rows.english: Therefore this conditionality by depending (paṭicca—ger.) makes states arise (uppādeti) equally and together (samaṃ saha ca), not piecemeal and successively—so it has been termed here thus by the Sage who is skilled in phraseology that conforms to its meaning: it has been accurately termed “dependent origination” (paṭicca samuppāda), is the meaning.

rows.pali: 580.Evaṃ bhāsamānena ca,
rows.english: 21.And while so termed:

rows.pali: Purimenāti paccayasāmaggiparidīpakena paṭiccapadena pavattidhammānaṃ paccayasāmaggiyaṃ āyattavuttittā sassatāhetuvisamahetuvasavattivādappabhedānaṃ sassatādīnaṃ abhāvo paridīpito hoti?
rows.english: The first: the word “dependent” (paṭicca) indicates the combination of the conditions, [522] since states in the process of occurring exist in dependence on the combining of their conditions; and it shows that they are not eternal, etc., thus denying the various doctrines of eternalism, no-cause, fictitious-cause, and power-wielder. 3

rows.pali: Kiṃ hi sassatānaṃ, ahetuādivasena vā pavattānaṃ paccayasāmaggiyāti?
rows.english: What purpose indeed would the combining of conditions serve, if things were eternal, or if they occurred without cause, and so on?

rows.pali: Pacchimena ca padenāti dhammānaṃ uppādaparidīpakena samuppādapadena paccayasāmaggiyaṃ dhammānaṃ uppattito vihatā ucchedanatthikaakiriyavādāti ucchedādivighāto paridīpito hoti.
rows.english: 23.The second: the word “origination” (samuppāda) indicates the arising of the states, since these occur when their conditions combine, and it shows how to prevent annihilationism, etc., thus preventing the various doctrines of annihilation [of a soul], nihilism, [“there is no use in giving,” etc.,] and moral-inefficacy-of- action, [“there is no other world,” etc.];

rows.pali: Purimapurimapaccayavasena hi punappunaṃ uppajjamānesu dhammesu kuto ucchedo, natthikākiriyavādā cāti.
rows.english: for when states [are seen to] arise again and again, each conditioned by its predecessor, how can the doctrines of annihilationism, nihilism, and moral-inefficacy-of-action be maintained?

rows.pali: Dvayenāti sakalena paṭiccasamuppādavacanena tassā tassā paccayasāmaggiyā santatiṃ avicchinditvā tesaṃ tesaṃ dhammānaṃ sambhavato majjhimā paṭipadā, "so karoti so paṭisaṃvedeti, añño karoti añño paṭisaṃvedetī"ti vādappahānaṃ, janapadaniruttiyā anabhiniveso, samaññāya anatidhāvananti ayaṃ ñāyo paridīpito hotīti ayaṃ tāva paṭiccasamuppādoti vacanamattassa attho.
rows.english: 24.The two together: since any given states are produced without interrupting the [cause-fruit] continuity of any given combination of conditions, the whole expression “dependent origination” (paṭicca-samuppāda) represents the middle way, which rejects the doctrines, “He who acts is he who reaps” and “One acts while another reaps” (S II 20), and which is the proper way described thus, “Not insisting on local language and not overriding normal usage” (M III 234). 4

rows.pali: 581.Yā panāyaṃ bhagavatā paṭiccasamuppādaṃ desentena "avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā"tiādinā nayena nikkhittā tanti, tassā atthasaṃvaṇṇanaṃ karontena vibhajjavādimaṇḍalaṃ otaritvā ācariye anabbhācikkhantena sakasamayaṃ avokkamantena parasamayaṃ anāyūhantena suttaṃ appaṭibāhantena vinayaṃ anulomentena mahāpadese olokentena dhammaṃ dīpentena atthaṃ saṅgāhentena tamevatthaṃ punarāvattetvā aparehipi pariyāyantarehi niddisantena ca yasmā atthasaṃvaṇṇanā kātabbā hoti, pakatiyāpi ca dukkarāva paṭiccasamuppādassa atthasaṃvaṇṇanā.
rows.english: 25.Now, in teaching this dependent origination the Blessed One has set forth the text in the way beginning, “With ignorance as condition there are formations” (S II 20). Its meaning should be commented on by one who keeps within the circle of the Vibhajjavādins,5 who does not misrepresent the teachers, who does not advertise his own standpoint, who does not quarrel with the standpoint of others, who does not distort suttas, who is in agreement with the Vinaya, who looks to the principal authorities (mahāpadesa—D II 123ff.), who illustrates the law (dhamma), who takes up the meaning (attha), repeatedly reverting to that same meaning, describing it in various different ways. 6 And it is inherently difficult to comment on the dependent origination,

rows.pali: Yathāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: as the Ancients said:

rows.pali: Tasmā aññatra āgamādhigamappattehi na sukarā paṭiccasamuppādassatthavaṇṇanāti paritulayitvā,
rows.english: Therefore, considering that to comment on the dependent origination is impossible except for those who are expert in the texts:

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ pubbācariyehi –
rows.english: 26.For this has been said by the former teachers:

rows.pali: 582.Iti avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārātiādīsu hi āditoyeva tāva,
rows.english: 27.So as regards the passages that begin: “With ignorance as condition there are formations” (S II 20), to start with:

rows.pali: Desanābhedato attha, lakkhaṇekavidhādito.
rows.english: (1) As different ways of teaching, (2) meaning, (3) Character, (4) singlefold and so on,

rows.pali: Aṅgānañca vavatthānā, viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: (5) As to defining of the factors, The exposition should be known.

rows.pali: Tattha desanābhedatoti bhagavato hi vallihārakānaṃ catunnaṃ purisānaṃ valligahaṇaṃ viya ādito vā majjhato vā paṭṭhāya yāva pariyosānaṃ, tathā pariyosānato vā majjhato vā paṭṭhāya yāva ādīti catubbidhā paṭiccasamuppādadesanā.
rows.english: 28. 1. Herein, as different ways of teaching: the Blessed One’s teaching of the dependent origination is fourfold, namely, (i) from the beginning; or (ii) from the middle up to the end; and (iii) from the end; or (iv) from the middle down to the beginning. It is like four creeper-gatherers’ ways of seizing a creeper.

rows.pali: Yathā hi vallihārakesu catūsu purisesu eko valliyā mūlameva paṭhamaṃ passati, so taṃ mūle chetvā sabbaṃ ākaḍḍhitvā ādāya kamme upaneti, evaṃ bhagavā "iti kho, bhikkhave, avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā - pe - jātipaccayā jarāmaraṇa"nti (ma. ni. 1.402; saṃ. ni. 2.2) ādito paṭṭhāya yāva pariyosānāpi paṭiccasamuppādaṃ deseti.
rows.english: 29.(i) For just as one of four men gathering creepers sees only the root of the creeper first, and after cutting it at the root, he pulls it all out and takes it away and uses it, so the Blessed One teaches the dependent origination from the beginning up to the end thus: “So, bhikkhus, with ignorance as condition there are formations; … with birth as condition ageing-and-death” (M I 261).

rows.pali: Yathā pana tesu purisesu eko valliyā majjhaṃ paṭhamaṃ passati, so majjhe chinditvā uparibhāgaññeva ākaḍḍhitvā ādāya kamme upaneti, evaṃ bhagavā "tassa taṃ vedanaṃ abhinandato abhivadato ajjhosāya tiṭṭhato uppajjati nandī.
rows.english: 30.(ii) Just as another of the four men sees the middle of the creeper first, and after cutting it in the middle, he pulls out only the upper part and takes it away and uses it. “When he is delighted with, welcomes, remains committed to that feeling, then delight arises in him.

rows.pali: Yā vedanāsu nandī, tadupādānaṃ.
rows.english: Delight in feelings is clinging.

rows.pali: Tassupādānapaccayā bhavo, bhavapaccayā jātī"ti (ma. ni. 1.409; saṃ. ni. 3.5) majjhato paṭṭhāya yāva pariyosānāpi deseti.
rows.english: With his clinging as condition there is becoming; with becoming as condition, birth” (M I 266). - so the Blessed One teaches it from the middle up to the end thus.

rows.pali: Yathā ca tesu purisesu eko valliyā aggaṃ paṭhamaṃ passati, so agge gahetvā aggānusārena yāva mūlā sabbaṃ ādāya kamme upaneti, evaṃ bhagavā "jātipaccayā jarāmaraṇanti iti kho panetaṃ vuttaṃ, jātipaccayā nu kho, bhikkhave, jarāmaraṇaṃ no vā kathaṃ vo ettha hotīti?
rows.english: 31. (iii) Just as another of the four men sees the tip of the creeper first, and seizing the tip, he follows it down to the root and takes all of it away and uses it. “‘With birth as condition, ageing-and-death,’ so it was said. But is there ageing-and- death with birth as condition, or not, or how is it here?

rows.pali: Jātipaccayā, bhante, jarāmaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: —There is ageing-and- death with birth as condition,

rows.pali: Evaṃ no ettha hoti jātipaccayā jarāmaraṇanti.
rows.english: so we think, venerable sir.

rows.pali: Bhavapaccayā jāti - pe - avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārāti iti kho panetaṃ vuttaṃ, avijjāpaccayā nu kho, bhikkhave, saṅkhārā no vā kathaṃ vo ettha hotī"ti (ma. ni. 1.403) pariyosānato paṭṭhāya yāva āditopi paṭiccasamuppādaṃ deseti.
rows.english: ‘With becoming as condition, birth,’ so it was said … ‘With ignorance as condition there are formations,’ so it was said. But are there formations with ignorance as condition, or not, or how is it here? —There are formations with ignorance as condition, so we think, venerable sir” (M I 261). - so the Blessed One teaches it from the end down to the beginning thus.

rows.pali: Yathā panetesu purisesu eko valliyā majjhameva paṭhamaṃ passati, so majjhe chinditvā heṭṭhā otaranto yāva mūlā ādāya kamme upaneti, evaṃ bhagavā "ime ca, bhikkhave, cattāro āhārā kinnidānā, kiṃsamudayā, kiṃjātikā, kiṃpabhavā?
rows.english: 32.(iv) Just as one of the four men sees only the middle of the creeper first, and after cutting it in the middle and tracing it down as far as the root, he takes it away and uses it. “And these four nutriments, bhikkhus: what is their source? What is their origin? From what are they born? By what are they produced?

rows.pali: Ime cattāro āhārā taṇhānidānā, taṇhāsamudayā, taṇhājātikā, taṇhāpabhavā.
rows.english: These four nutriments have craving as their source, craving as their origin, they are born from craving, produced by craving.

rows.pali: Taṇhā kinnidānā… vedanā… phasso… saḷāyatanaṃ… nāmarūpaṃ… viññāṇaṃ… saṅkhārā kinnidānā - pe - saṅkhārā avijjānidānā - pe - avijjāpabhavā"ti (saṃ. ni. 2.11) majjhato paṭṭhāya yāva ādito deseti.
rows.english: Craving: what is its source? … Feeling: … Contact: … The sixfold base: … Mentality-materiality: … Consciousness: … Formations: what is their source? … By what are they produced? Formations have ignorance as their source … they are … produced by ignorance” (S II 11f.). so the Blessed One teaches it from the middle down to the beginning thus.

rows.pali: 583.Kasmā panevaṃ desetīti?
rows.english: 33. Why does he teach it thus?

rows.pali: Paṭiccasamuppādassa samantabhaddakattā sayañca desanāvilāsappattattā.
rows.english: Because the dependent origination is wholly beneficial and because he has himself acquired elegance in instructing.

rows.pali: Samantabhaddako hi paṭiccasamuppādo, tato tato ñāyapaṭivedhāya saṃvattatiyeva.
rows.english: For the dependent origination is entirely beneficial: starting from any one of the four starting points, it leads only to the penetration of the proper way.

rows.pali: Desanāvilāsappatto ca bhagavā catuvesārajjapaṭisambhidāyogena catubbidhagambhīrabhāvappattiyā ca.
rows.english: And the Blessed One has acquired elegance in instructing: it is because he has done so through possession of the four kinds of perfect confidence and the four discriminations and by achieving the fourfold profundity (§304)

rows.pali: So desanāvilāsappattattā nānānayeheva dhammaṃ deseti.
rows.english: that he teaches the Dhamma by various methods.

rows.pali: Visesato panassa yā ādito paṭṭhāya anulomadesanā, sā pavattikāraṇavibhāgasaṃmūḷhaṃ veneyyajanaṃ samanupassato yathāsakehi kāraṇehi pavattisandassanatthaṃ uppattikkamasandassanatthañca pavattāti viññātabbā.
rows.english: 34.But it should be recognized, in particular, that (i) when he sees that people susceptible of teaching are confused about the analysis of the causes of the process [of becoming], he employs his teaching of it forwards starting from the beginning in order to show that the process carries on according to its own peculiar laws and for the purpose of showing the order of arising.

rows.pali: Yā pariyosānato paṭṭhāya paṭilomadesanā, sā "kicchaṃ vatāyaṃ loko āpanno jāyati ca jīyati ca mīyati ca cavati ca upapajjati cā"tiādinā (dī. ni. 2.57; saṃ. ni. 2.4) nayena kicchāpannaṃ lokaṃ anuvilokayato pubbabhāgapaṭivedhānusārena tassa tassa jarāmaraṇādikassa dukkhassa attanā adhigatakāraṇasandassanatthaṃ.
rows.english: (iii) And it should be recognized that when he surveys the world as fallen upon trouble in the way stated thus, “This world has fallen upon trouble; it is born, ages, dies, passes away, and reappears” (S II 10), he employs his teaching of it backwards starting from the end in order to show the [laws governing the] various kinds of suffering beginning with ageing and death, which he discovered himself in the early stage of his penetration.

rows.pali: Yā majjhato paṭṭhāya yāva ādi pavattā, sā āhāranidānavavatthāpanānusārena yāva atītaṃ addhānaṃ atiharitvā puna atītaddhato pabhuti hetuphalapaṭipāṭisandassanatthaṃ.
rows.english: And (iv) it should be recognized that he employs his teaching of it backwards from the middle down to the beginning in order to show how the succession of cause and fruit extends back into the past [existence], and again forwards from the past, in accordance with his definition of nutriment as the source [of ignorance] (see M I 47f.).

rows.pali: Yā pana majjhato paṭṭhāya yāva pariyosānaṃ pavattā, sā paccuppanne addhāne anāgataddhahetusamuṭṭhānato pabhuti anāgataddhasandassanatthaṃ.
rows.english: And (ii) it should be recognized that he employs his teaching of it forwards from the middle up to the end in order to show how the future [existence] follows on [through rebirth] from arousing in the present causes for [rebirth] in the future.

rows.pali: Tāsu yā pavattikāraṇasammūḷhassa veneyyajanassa yathāsakehi kāraṇehi pavattisandassanatthaṃ uppattikkamasandassanatthañca ādito paṭṭhāya anulomadesanā vuttā, sā idha nikkhittāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 35.Of these methods of presentation, that cited here should be understood to be that stated in forward order starting from the beginning in order to show to people susceptible of teaching who are confused about the laws of the process [of becoming] that the process carries on according to its own peculiar laws, [525] and for the purpose of showing the order of arising.

rows.pali: 584.Kasmā panettha avijjā ādito vuttā, kiṃ pakativādīnaṃ pakati viya avijjāpi akāraṇaṃ mūlakāraṇaṃ lokassāti?
rows.english: 36.But why is ignorance stated as the beginning here? How then, is ignorance the causeless root-cause of the world like the Primordial Essence of those who assert the existence of a Primordial Essence?

rows.pali: Na akāraṇaṃ.
rows.english: It is not causeless.

rows.pali: "Āsavasamudayā avijjāsamudayo"ti (ma. ni. 1.103) hi avijjāya kāraṇaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: For a cause of ignorance is stated thus, “With the arising of cankers there is the arising of ignorance” (M I 54).

rows.pali: Atthi pana pariyāyo yena mūlakāraṇaṃ siyā, ko pana soti?
rows.english: But there is a figurative way in which it can be treated as the root cause. What way is that?

rows.pali: Vaṭṭakathāya sīsabhāvo.
rows.english: When it is made to serve as a starting point in an exposition of the round [of becoming].

rows.pali: Bhagavā hi vaṭṭakathaṃ kathento dve dhamme sīsaṃ katvā katheti, avijjaṃ vā.
rows.english: 37. For the Blessed One gives the exposition of the round with one of two things as the starting point: either ignorance,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "purimā, bhikkhave, koṭi na paññāyati avijjāya 'ito pubbe avijjā nāhosi, atha pacchā samabhavī'ti, evañcetaṃ, bhikkhave, vuccati, atha ca pana paññāyati idappaccayā avijjā"ti (a. ni. 10.61).
rows.english: according as it is said, “No first beginning of ignorance is made known, bhikkhus, before which there was no ignorance, and after which there came to be ignorance. And while it is said thus, bhikkhus, nevertheless it is made known that ignorance has its specific condition” (A V 113);

rows.pali: Bhavataṇhaṃ vā.
rows.english: or craving for becoming,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "purimā, bhikkhave, koṭi na paññāyati bhavataṇhāya 'ito pubbe bhavataṇhā nāhosi, atha pacchā samabhavī'ti, evañcetaṃ, bhikkhave, vuccati, atha ca pana paññāyati idappaccayā bhavataṇhā"ti (a. ni. 10.62).
rows.english: according as it is said, “No first beginning of craving for becoming is made known, bhikkhus, before which there was no craving for becoming, and after which there came to be craving for becoming. And while it is said thus, bhikkhus, nevertheless it is made known that craving for becoming has its specific condition” (A V 116).

rows.pali: 585.Kasmā pana bhagavā vaṭṭakathaṃ kathento ime dve dhamme sīsaṃ katvā kathetīti?
rows.english: 38.But why does the Blessed One give the exposition of the round with those two things as starting points?

rows.pali: Sugatiduggatigāmino kammassa visesahetubhūtattā.
rows.english: Because they are the outstanding causes of kamma that leads to happy and unhappy destinies.

rows.pali: Duggatigāmino hi kammassa visesahetu avijjā.
rows.english: 39. Ignorance is an outstanding cause of kamma that leads to unhappy destinies.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Yasmā avijjābhibhūto puthujjano aggisantāpalaguḷābhighātaparissamābhibhūtā vajjhagāvī tāya parissamāturatāya nirassādampi attano anatthāvahampi ca uṇhodakapānaṃ viya kilesasantāpato nirassādampi duggatinipātanato ca attano anatthāvahampi pāṇātipātādiṃ anekappakāraṃ duggatigāmikammaṃ ārabhati.
rows.english: Because, just as when a cow to be slaughtered is in the grip of the torment of burning with fire and belabouring with cudgels, and being crazed with torment, she drinks the hot water although it gives no satisfaction and does her harm, so the ordinary man who is in the grip of ignorance performs kamma of the various kinds beginning with killing living things that leads to unhappy destinies, although it gives no satisfaction because of the burning of defilements and does him harm because it casts him into an unhappy destiny.

rows.pali: Sugatigāmino pana kammassa visesahetu bhavataṇhā.
rows.english: 40.But craving for becoming is an outstanding cause of kamma that leads to happy destinies.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Yasmā bhavataṇhābhibhūto puthujjano sā vuttappakārā gāvī sītūdakataṇhāya saassādaṃ attano parissamavinodanañca sītūdakapānaṃ viya kilesasantāpavirahato saassādaṃ sugatisampāpanena attano duggatidukkhaparissamavinodanañca pāṇātipātā veramaṇiādiṃ anekappakāraṃ sugatigāmikammaṃ ārabhati.
rows.english: Because, just as that same cow, through her craving for cold water, starts drinking cold water, which gives satisfaction and allays her torment, so the ordinary man in the grip of craving for becoming performs kamma of the various kinds beginning with abstention from killing living things that leads to happy destinies and gives satisfaction because it is free from the burning of defilements and, by bringing him to a happy destiny, allays the torment of suffering [experienced] in the unhappy destinies.

rows.pali: 586.Etesu pana vaṭṭakathāya sīsabhūtesu dhammesu katthaci bhagavā ekadhammamūlikaṃ desanaṃ deseti.
rows.english: 41.Now, as regards these two states that are starting points in expositions of the process [of becoming], in some instances the Blessed One teaches the Dhamma based on a single one of these states,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, "iti kho, bhikkhave, avijjūpanisā saṅkhārā, saṅkhārūpanisaṃ viññāṇa"ntiādi (saṃ. ni. 2.23).
rows.english: for instance, [526] “Accordingly, bhikkhus, formations have ignorance as their cause, consciousness has formations as its cause” (S II 31), etc.;

rows.pali: Tathā "upādāniyesu, bhikkhave, dhammesu assādānupassino viharato taṇhā pavaḍḍhati, taṇhāpaccayā upādāna"ntiādi (saṃ. ni. 2.52).
rows.english: likewise, “Bhikkhus, craving increases in one who dwells seeing enjoyment in things productive of clinging; with craving as condition there is clinging” (S II 84), and so on.

rows.pali: Katthaci ubhayamūlikampi.
rows.english: In some instances he does so based on both,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, "avijjānīvaraṇassa, bhikkhave, bālassa taṇhāya sampayuttassa evamayaṃ kāyo samudāgato.
rows.english: for instance: “So, bhikkhus, for the fool who is hindered by ignorance and tethered by craving there arises this body.

rows.pali: Iti ayañceva kāyo bahiddhā ca nāmarūpaṃ itthetaṃ dvayaṃ.
rows.english: Now, this body [with its six internal bases] and externally [the six bases due to] mentality-materiality make a duality.

rows.pali: Dvayaṃ paṭicca phasso saḷevāyatanāni, yehi phuṭṭho bālo sukhadukkhaṃ paṭisaṃvedetī"tiādi (saṃ. ni. 2.19).
rows.english: Due to this duality there is contact, as well as the six [pairs of] bases, touched through which the fool feels pleasure and pain” (S II 23f.), and so on.

rows.pali: Tāsu desanāsu "avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā"ti ayamidha avijjāvasena ekadhammamūlikā desanāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 42.Of these ways of presentation, that cited here in the form “With ignorance as condition there are formations” should be understood as one based on a single state.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāvettha desanābhedato viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: This, firstly, is how the exposition should be known “as to different ways of teaching. ”

rows.pali: 587.Atthatoti avijjādīnaṃ padānaṃ atthato.
rows.english: 43.2.As to meaning: as to the meaning of the words “ignorance” and so on.

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, pūretuṃ ayuttaṭṭhena kāyaduccaritādi avindiyaṃ nāma, aladdhabbanti attho.
rows.english: Bodily misconduct, etc., for example, “ought not to be found” (avindiya), in the sense of being unfit to be carried out; the meaning is that it should not be permitted.

rows.pali: Taṃ avindiyaṃ vindatīti avijjā.
rows.english: It finds (vindati) what ought not to be found (avindiya), thus it is ignorance (avijjā).

rows.pali: Tabbiparītato kāyasucaritādi vindiyaṃ nāma, taṃ vindiyaṃ na vindatīti avijjā.
rows.english: Conversely, good bodily conduct, etc. “ought to be found” (vindiya).

rows.pali: Khandhānaṃ rāsaṭṭhaṃ, āyatanānaṃ āyatanaṭṭhaṃ, dhātūnaṃ suññaṭṭhaṃ, indriyānaṃ adhipatiyaṭṭhaṃ, saccānaṃ tathaṭṭhaṃ aviditaṃ karotītipi avijjā.
rows.english: It does not find (na vindati) what ought to be found (vindiya), thus it is ignorance (avijjā). Also it prevents knowing (avidita) the meaning of collection in the aggregates, the meaning of actuating in the bases, the meaning of voidness in the elements, the meaning of predominance in the faculties, the meaning of reality in the truths, thus it is ignorance (avijjā).

rows.pali: Dukkhādīnaṃ pīḷanādivasena vuttaṃ catubbidhaṃ atthaṃ aviditaṃ karotītipi avijjā.
rows.english: Also it prevents knowing the meaning of suffering, etc., described in four ways as “oppression,” etc. (XVI.15), thus it is ignorance.

rows.pali: Antavirahite saṃsāre sabbayonigatibhavaviññāṇaṭṭhitisattāvāsesu satte javāpetīti avijjā.
rows.english: Through all the kinds of generations, destinies, becoming, stations of consciousness, and abodes of beings in the endless round of rebirths it drives beings on (AntaVIrahite saṃsāre … satte JAvāpeti), thus it is ignorance (avijjā).

rows.pali: Paramatthato avijjamānesu itthipurisādīsu javati, vijjamānesupi khandhādīsu na javatīti avijjā.
rows.english: Amongst women, men, etc., which are in the ultimate sense non-existent, it hurries on (paramatthato AVIJjamānesu itthi-purisādisu JAvati), and amongst the aggregates, etc., which are existent, it does not hurry on (vijjamānesu pi khandhādisu na javati), thus it is ignorance (avijjā).

rows.pali: Apica cakkhuviññāṇādīnaṃ vatthārammaṇānaṃ paṭiccasamuppādapaṭiccasamuppannānañca dhammānaṃ chādanatopi avijjā.
rows.english: Furthermore, it is ignorance because it conceals the physical bases and objects of eye-consciousness, etc., and the dependent origination and dependently-originated states.

rows.pali: Yaṃ paṭicca phalameti, so paccayo.
rows.english: 44.That due to (paṭicca) which fruit comes (eti) is a condition (paccaya).

rows.pali: Paṭiccāti na vinā apaccakkhatvāti attho.
rows.english: “Due to” (paṭicca) = “not without that”; the meaning is, not dispensing with it.

rows.pali: Etīti uppajjati ceva pavattati cāti attho.
rows.english: “Comes” (eti) means both “arises” and “occurs.”

rows.pali: Apica upakārakaṭṭho paccayaṭṭho.
rows.english: Furthermore, the meaning of “condition” is the meaning of “help."

rows.pali: Avijjā ca sā paccayo cāti avijjāpaccayo.
rows.english: It is ignorance and that is a condition, thus it is “ignorance as condition,”

rows.pali: Tasmā avijjāpaccayā.
rows.english: whence the phrase “with ignorance as condition.”

rows.pali: Saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharontīti saṅkhārā.
rows.english: “They form the formed” (S III 87), thus they are formations.

rows.pali: Apica avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā, saṅkhārasaddena āgatasaṅkhārāti duvidhā saṅkhārā.
rows.english: Furthermore, formations are twofold, namely, (a) formations with ignorance as condition, and (b) formations given in the texts with the word “formations” (saṅkhāra).

rows.pali: Tattha puññāpuññāneñjābhisaṅkhārā tayo, kāyavacīcittasaṅkhārā tayoti ime cha avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā.
rows.english: Herein, (a) the three, namely, formations of merit, of demerit, and of the imperturbable, and the three, namely, the bodily, the verbal, and the mental formations, which make six, are “formations with ignorance as condition."

rows.pali: Te sabbepi lokiyakusalākusalacetanāmattameva honti.
rows.english: And all these are simply mundane profitable and unprofitable volition.

rows.pali: Saṅkhatasaṅkhāro, abhisaṅkhatasaṅkhāro, abhisaṅkharaṇakasaṅkhāro, payogābhisaṅkhāroti ime pana cattāro saṅkhāra-saddena āgatasaṅkhārā.
rows.english: 45. But (b) these four, namely, (i) the formation consisting of the formed (saṅkhata-saṅkhāra), [527] (ii) the formation consisting of the kamma-formed (abhisaṅkhata-saṅkhāra), (iii) the formation consisting in the act of kamma-forming (forming by kamma—abhisaṅkharaṇa-saṅkhāra), and (iv) the formation consisting in momentum (payogābhisaṅkhāra), are the kinds of formations that have come in the texts with the word “formations. ”

rows.pali: Tattha "aniccā vata saṅkhārā"tiādīsu (dī. ni. 2.221, 272; saṃ. ni. 1.186) vuttā sabbepi sappaccayā dhammā saṅkhatasaṅkhārā nāma.
rows.english: 46.Herein, (i) all states with conditions, given in such passages as “Formations are impermanent” (S I 158; D II 157), are formations consisting of the formed.

rows.pali: Kammanibbattā tebhūmakā rūpārūpadhammā abhisaṅkhatasaṅkhārāti aṭṭhakathāsu vuttā, tepi "aniccā vata saṅkhārā"ti (dī. ni. 2.221; 272; saṃ. ni. 1.186) ettheva saṅgahaṃ gacchanti.
rows.english: (ii) In the Commentaries material and immaterial states of the three planes generated by kamma are called formations consisting of the kamma-formed. These are also included in the passage, “Formations are impermanent.”

rows.pali: Visuṃ pana nesaṃ āgataṭṭhānaṃ na paññāyati.
rows.english: But there is no instance in the texts where they are found separately.

rows.pali: Tebhūmikakusalākusalacetanā pana abhisaṅkharaṇakasaṅkhāroti vuccati, tassa "avijjāgatoyaṃ, bhikkhave, purisapuggalo puññañceva saṅkhāraṃ abhisaṅkharotī"tiādīsu (saṃ. ni. 2.51) āgataṭṭhānaṃ paññāyati.
rows.english: (iii) Profitable and unprofitable volition of the three planes is called the formation consisting in the act of kamma-forming. It is found in the texts in such passages as “Bhikkhus, this man in his ignorance forms the formation of merit” (S II 82).

rows.pali: Kāyikacetasikaṃ pana vīriyaṃ payogābhisaṅkhāroti vuccati, so "yāvatikā abhisaṅkhārassa gati, tāvatikā gantvā akkhāhataṃ maññe aṭṭhāsī"tiādīsu (a. ni. 3.15) āgato.
rows.english: (iv) But it is bodily and mental energy that is called the formation consisting in momentum. This is given in the texts in such passages as “The wheel, having gone as far as the impetus (abhisaṅkhāra) carried it, stood as though it were fixed” (A I 112).

rows.pali: Na kevalañca eteyeva, aññepi "saññāvedayitanirodhaṃ samāpajjantassa kho, āvuso visākha, bhikkhuno paṭhamaṃ nirujjhati vacīsaṅkhāro, tato kāyasaṅkhāro, tato cittasaṅkhāro"tiādinā (ma. ni. 1.464) nayena saṅkhāra-saddena āgatā aneke saṅkhārā.
rows.english: 47. And not only these, but many other kinds of formations are given in the texts with the word “formation” (saṅkhāra), in the way beginning, “When a bhikkhu is attaining the cessation of perception and feeling, friend Visākha, first his verbal formation ceases, then his bodily formation, then his mental formation” (M I 302).

rows.pali: Tesu natthi so saṅkhāro, yo saṅkhatasaṅkhārehi saṅgahaṃ na gaccheyya, ito paraṃ saṅkhārapaccayā viññāṇantiādīsu vuttaṃ vuttanayeneva veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: But there is no formation among them not included by (i) “formations consisting of the formed.” 48.What is said next after this in the [rest of the exposition] beginning, “With formations as condition, consciousness” should be understood in the way already stated.

rows.pali: Avutte pana vijānātīti viññāṇaṃ.
rows.english: But as to those words not yet dealt with: It cognizes (vijānāti), thus it is consciousness (viññāṇa—see M I 292).

rows.pali: Namatīti nāmaṃ.
rows.english: It bends [towards an object] (namati), thus it is mentality (nāma).

rows.pali: Ruppatīti rūpaṃ.
rows.english: It is molested (ruppati), thus it is materiality (rūpa—see S III 87).

rows.pali: Āye tanoti āyatañca nayatīti āyatanaṃ.
rows.english: It provides a range for the origins (āye tanoti) and it leads on what is actuated (āyatañ ca nayati), thus it is a base (āyatana—see XV.4).

rows.pali: Phusatīti phasso.
rows.english: It touches (phusati), thus it is contact (phassa).

rows.pali: Vedayatīti vedanā.
rows.english: It is felt (vedayati), thus it is feeling (vedanā— see M I 293).

rows.pali: Paritassatīti taṇhā.
rows.english: It frets (or it thirsts—paritassati), thus it is craving (taṇhā).

rows.pali: Upādiyatīti upādānaṃ.
rows.english: It clings (upādiyati), thus it is clinging (upādāna).

rows.pali: Bhavati bhāvayati cāti bhavo.
rows.english: It becomes (bhavati) and it makes become (bhāvayati), thus it is becoming (bhava).

rows.pali: Jananaṃ jāti.
rows.english: The act of being born is birth.

rows.pali: Jiraṇaṃ jarā.
rows.english: The act of growing old is ageing.

rows.pali: Maranti etenāti maraṇaṃ.
rows.english: By means of it they die, thus it is death.

rows.pali: Socanaṃ soko.
rows.english: The act of sorrowing is sorrow.

rows.pali: Paridevanaṃ paridevo.
rows.english: The act of lamenting is lamentation.

rows.pali: Dukkhayatīti dukkhaṃ.
rows.english: It makes [beings] suffer (dukkhayati), thus it is pain (dukkha);

rows.pali: Uppādaṭṭhitivasena vā dvidhā khaṇatītipi dukkhaṃ.
rows.english: or it consumes in two ways (DVedhā KHAṇati—see IV.100) by means of [the two moments (khaṇa)] arising and presence, thus it is pain (dukkha).

rows.pali: Dummanabhāvo domanassaṃ.
rows.english: The state of a sad mind (dummana-bhāva) is grief (domanassa).

rows.pali: Bhuso āyāso upāyāso.
rows.english: Great misery (bhuso āyāso) is despair (upāyāsa).

rows.pali: Sambhavantīti abhinibbattanti.
rows.english: There is means “is generated. ”

rows.pali: Na kevalañca sokādīheva, atha kho sabbapadehi sambhavanti-saddassa yojanā kātabbā.
rows.english: 49.And the words “There is” should be construed with all the terms, not only with those beginning with sorrow;

rows.pali: Itarathā hi "avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā"ti vutte kiṃ karontīti na paññāyeyya, sambhavantīti pana yojanāya sati avijjā ca sā paccayo cāti avijjāpaccayo.
rows.english: for otherwise, when “With ignorance as condition, formations” was said, it would not be evident what they did, but by construing it with the words “There is” (or “there are”), since “ignorance as condition” stands for “it is ignorance and that is a condition,”

rows.pali: Tasmā avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārā sambhavantīti paccayapaccayuppannavavatthānaṃ kataṃ hoti.
rows.english: consequently [528] the defining of the condition and the conditionally-arisen state is effected by the words “with ignorance as condition there are formations.”

rows.pali: Esa nayo sabbattha.
rows.english: And so in each instance.

rows.pali: Evanti niddiṭṭhanayanidassanaṃ.
rows.english: 50. Thus signifies the process described.

rows.pali: Tena avijjādīheva kāraṇehi, na issaranimmānādīhīti dasseti.
rows.english: By that he shows that it is with ignorance, etc., as the causes and not with creation by an Overlord, and so on.

rows.pali: Etassāti yathāvuttassa.
rows.english: Of that: of that aforesaid.

rows.pali: Kevalassāti asammissassa, sakalassa vā.
rows.english: Whole: unmixed, entire.

rows.pali: Dukkhakkhandhassāti dukkhasamūhassa, na sattassa, na sukhasubhādīnaṃ.
rows.english: Mass of suffering: totality of suffering; not a living being, not pleasure, beauty, and so on.

rows.pali: Samudayoti nibbatti.
rows.english: Arising: generating.

rows.pali: Hotīti sambhavati.
rows.english: There is: is brought about.

rows.pali: Evamettha atthato viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: This is how the exposition should be known here “as to meaning. ”

rows.pali: 588.Lakkhaṇāditoti avijjādīnaṃ lakkhaṇādito.
rows.english: 51. 3. As to character, etc.: as to the characteristics of ignorance, etc.,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – aññāṇalakkhaṇā avijjā, sammohanarasā, chādanapaccupaṭṭhānā, āsavapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: that is to say, ignorance has the characteristic of unknowing. Its function is to confuse. It is manifested as concealing. Its proximate cause is cankers.

rows.pali: Abhisaṅkharaṇalakkhaṇā saṅkhārā, āyūhanarasā, cetanāpaccupaṭṭhānā, avijjāpadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: Formations have the characteristic of forming. Their function is to accumulate. 7 They are manifested as volition. Their proximate cause is ignorance.

rows.pali: Vijānanalakkhaṇaṃ viññāṇaṃ, pubbaṅgamarasaṃ, paṭisandhipaccupaṭṭhānaṃ, saṅkhārapadaṭṭhānaṃ, vatthārammaṇapadaṭṭhānaṃ vā.
rows.english: Consciousness has the characteristic of cognizing. Its function is to go before (see Dhp 1). It manifests itself as rebirth-linking. Its proximate cause is formations; or its proximate cause is the physical-basis-cum-object.

rows.pali: Namanalakkhaṇaṃ nāmaṃ, sampayogarasaṃ, avinibbhogapaccupaṭṭhānaṃ, viññāṇapadaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Mentality (nāma) has the characteristic of bending (namana). Its function is to associate. It is manifested as inseparability of its components, [that is, the three aggregates]. Its proximate cause is consciousness.

rows.pali: Ruppanalakkhaṇaṃ rūpaṃ, vikiraṇarasaṃ, abyākatapaccupaṭṭhānaṃ, viññāṇapadaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Materiality (rūpa) has the characteristic of being molested (ruppana). Its function is to be dispersed. It is manifested as [morally] indeterminate. Its proximate cause is consciousness.

rows.pali: Āyatanalakkhaṇaṃ saḷāyatanaṃ, dassanādirasaṃ, vatthudvārabhāvapaccupaṭṭhānaṃ, nāmarūpapadaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: The sixfold base (saḷāyatana) has the characteristic of actuating (āyatana). Its function is to see, and so on. It is manifested as the state of physical basis and door. Its proximate cause is mentality-materiality.

rows.pali: Phusanalakkhaṇo phasso, saṅghaṭṭanaraso, saṅgatipaccupaṭṭhāno, saḷāyatanapadaṭṭhāno.
rows.english: Contact has the characteristic of touching. Its function is impingement. It manifests itself as coincidence [of internal and external base and consciousness]. Its proximate cause is the sixfold base.

rows.pali: Anubhavanalakkhaṇā vedanā, visayarasasambhogarasā, sukhadukkhapaccupaṭṭhānā, phassapadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: Feeling has the characteristic of experiencing. Its function is to exploit the stimulus of the objective field. It is manifested as pleasure and pain. Its proximate cause is contact.

rows.pali: Hetulakkhaṇā taṇhā, abhinandanarasā, atittabhāvapaccupaṭṭhānā, vedanāpadaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: Craving has the characteristic of being a cause [that is, of suffering]. Its function is to delight. It is manifested as insatiability. Its proximate cause is feeling.

rows.pali: Gahaṇalakkhaṇaṃ upādānaṃ, amuñcanarasaṃ, taṇhādaḷhattadiṭṭhipaccupaṭṭhānaṃ, taṇhāpadaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Clinging has the characteristic of seizing. Its function is not to release. It is manifested as a strong form of craving and as [false] view. Its proximate cause is craving.

rows.pali: Kammakammaphalalakkhaṇo bhavo, bhāvanabhavanaraso, kusalākusalābyākatapaccupaṭṭhāno, upādānapadaṭṭhāno.
rows.english: Becoming has the characteristic of being kamma and kamma-result. Its function is to make become and to become. It is manifested as profitable, unprofitable, and indeterminate. Its proximate cause is clinging.

rows.pali: Jātiādīnaṃ lakkhaṇādīni saccaniddese vuttanayeneva veditabbāni.
rows.english: The characteristic of birth, etc., should be understood as stated in the Description of the Truths (XVI.32f.).

rows.pali: Evamettha lakkhaṇāditopi viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: This is how the exposition should be known here “as to character, etc.”

rows.pali: 589.Ekavidhāditoti ettha avijjā aññāṇādassanamohādibhāvato ekavidhā.
rows.english: 52. 4. As to singlefold, and so on: here ignorance is singlefold as unknowing, unseeing, delusion, and so on.

rows.pali: Appaṭipattimicchāpaṭipattito duvidhā.
rows.english: It is twofold as “no theory” and “wrong theory” (cf. §303);8

rows.pali: Tathā sasaṅkhārāsaṅkhārato.
rows.english: likewise as prompted and unprompted.

rows.pali: Vedanattayasampayogato tividhā.
rows.english: It is threefold as associated with the three kinds of feeling.

rows.pali: Catusaccapaṭivedhato catubbidhā.
rows.english: It is fourfold as non-penetration of the four truths.

rows.pali: Gatipañcakādīnavacchādanato pañcavidhā.
rows.english: It is fivefold as concealing the danger in the five kinds of destinies.

rows.pali: Dvārārammaṇato pana sabbesupi arūpadhammesu chabbidhatā veditabbā.
rows.english: It should, however, be understood that all the immaterial factors [of the dependent origination] have a sixfold nature with respect to the [six] doors and objects.

rows.pali: Saṅkhārā sāsavavipākadhammadhammādibhāvato ekavidhā.
rows.english: 53.Formations are singlefold as states subject to cankers (Dhs 3), states with the nature of result (Dhs 1), and so on (cf. Vibh 62).9

rows.pali: Kusalākusalato duvidhā.
rows.english: They are twofold as profitable and unprofitable;

rows.pali: Tathā parittamahaggatahīnamajjhimamicchattaniyatāniyatato.
rows.english: likewise as limited and exalted, inferior and medium, with certainty of wrongness and without certainty.

rows.pali: Tividhā puññābhisaṅkhārādibhāvato.
rows.english: They are threefold as the formation of merit and the rest.

rows.pali: Catubbidhā catuyonisaṃvattanato.
rows.english: They are fourfold as leading to the four kinds of generation.

rows.pali: Pañcavidhā pañcagatigāmito.
rows.english: They are fivefold as leading to the five kinds of destiny.

rows.pali: Viññāṇaṃ lokiyavipākādibhāvato ekavidhaṃ.
rows.english: 54. Consciousness is singlefold as mundane (Dhs 3), resultant (Dhs 1), and so on.

rows.pali: Sahetukāhetukādito duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: It is twofold as with root-cause and without root-cause and so on.

rows.pali: Bhavattayapariyāpannato, vedanattayasampayogato, ahetukadvihetukatihetukato ca tividhaṃ.
rows.english: It is threefold as included in the three kinds of becoming; as associated with the three kinds of feeling; and as having no root-cause, having two root-causes, and having three root-causes.

rows.pali: Yonigativasena catubbidhaṃ, pañcavidhañca.
rows.english: It is fourfold and fivefold [respectively] according to generation and destiny.

rows.pali: Nāmarūpaṃ viññāṇasannissayato kammapaccayato ca ekavidhaṃ.
rows.english: 55. Mentality-materiality is singlefold as dependent on consciousness, and as having kamma as its condition.

rows.pali: Sārammaṇanārammaṇato duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: It is twofold as having an object [in the case of mentality], and having no object [in the case of materiality].

rows.pali: Atītādito tividhaṃ.
rows.english: It is threefold as past, and so on.

rows.pali: Yonigativasena catubbidhaṃ, pañcavidhañca.
rows.english: It is fourfold and fivefold respectively according to generation and destiny.

rows.pali: Saḷāyatanaṃ sañjātisamosaraṇaṭṭhānato ekavidhaṃ.
rows.english: 56.The sixfold base is singlefold as the place of origin and meeting.

rows.pali: Bhūtappasādaviññāṇādito duvidhaṃ.
rows.english: It is twofold as sensitivity of primary elements and as consciousness [of the sixth base], and so on.

rows.pali: Sampattāsampattanobhayagocarato tividhaṃ.
rows.english: It is threefold as having for its domain [objective fields that are] contiguous, non-contiguous, and neither (see XIV.46).

rows.pali: Yonigatipariyāpannato catubbidhaṃ pañcavidhañcāti iminā nayena phassādīnampi ekavidhādibhāvo veditabboti evamettha ekavidhāditopi viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: It is fourfold and fivefold respectively as included in the kinds of generation and destiny. The singlefoldness, etc., of contact, etc., should be understood in this way too. This is how the exposition should be known here “as to singlefold and so on. ”

rows.pali: 590.Aṅgānañca vavatthānāti sokādayo cettha bhavacakkassa avicchedadassanatthaṃ vuttā.
rows.english: 57. 5. As to defining of the factors: sorrow, etc., are stated here for the purpose of showing that the Wheel of Becoming never halts;

rows.pali: Jarāmaraṇabbhāhatassa hi bālassa te sambhavanti.
rows.english: for they are produced in the fool who is afflicted by ageing and death,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "assutavā, bhikkhave, puthujjano sārīrikāya dukkhāya vedanāya phuṭṭho samāno socati kilamati paridevati urattāḷiṃ kandati sammohamāpajjatī"ti (saṃ. ni. 4.252).
rows.english: according as it is said: “The untaught ordinary man, bhikkhus, on being touched by painful bodily feeling, sorrows, grieves and laments, beating his breast, he weeps and becomes distraught” (M III 285; S IV 206).

rows.pali: Yāva ca tesaṃ pavatti, tāva avijjāyāti punapi avijjāpaccayā saṅkhārāti sambandhameva hoti bhavacakkaṃ.
rows.english: And as long as these go on occurring so long does ignorance, and so the Wheel of Becoming renews [its revolution]: “With ignorance as condition there are formations” and so on.

rows.pali: Tasmā tesaṃ jarāmaraṇeneva ekasaṅkhepaṃ katvā dvādaseva paṭiccasamuppādaṅgānīti veditabbāni.
rows.english: That is why the factors of the dependent origination should be understood as twelve by taking those [that is, sorrow, etc.,] along with ageing-and-death as one summarization.

rows.pali: Evamettha aṅgānaṃ vavatthānatopi viññātabbo vinicchayo.
rows.english: This is how the exposition should be known here “as to defining of the factors.”

rows.pali: 561.Ayaṃ pana vitthāranayo – avijjāti suttantapariyāyena dukkhādīsu catūsu ṭhānesu aññāṇaṃ, abhidhammapariyāyena pubbantādīhi saddhiṃ aṭṭhasu.
rows.english: The following method, however, is in detail. According to the Suttanta method ignorance is unknowing about the four instances beginning with suffering. According to the Abhidhamma method it is unknowing about the eight instances [that is to say, the above-mentioned four] together with [the four] beginning with the past;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "tattha katamā avijjā, dukkhe aññāṇaṃ - pe - dukkhanirodhagāminiyā paṭipadāya aññāṇaṃ, pubbante aññāṇaṃ, aparante, pubbantāparante, idappaccayatāpaṭiccasamuppannesu dhammesu aññāṇa"nti (dha. sa. 1106).
rows.english: for this is said: “Herein, what is ignorance?
